## 4.1 Block Diagram — Biomechanics of Motion Analytical Framework

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the interrelationship between the core analytical components of biomechanics of motion and the safety applications that depend on them.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HUMAN BODY MODEL] --> B[Anthropometry]
    A --> C[Body Segment Parameters]
    A --> D[Joint Geometry]

    B --> E[Kinematics]
    C --> E
    D --> E

    B --> F[Kinetics]
    C --> F
    D --> F

    E --> G[Displacement, Velocity, Acceleration]
    E --> H[Joint Angles and ROM]

    F --> I[Forces on Joints]
    F --> J[Muscle Torques]
    F --> K[Disc Compression Loads]

    G --> L[Biomechanical Analysis Output]
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L

    L --> M[NIOSH Lifting Equation]
    L --> N[OWAS REBA RULA Posture Assessment]
    L --> O[Human Strength Limits]
    L --> P[Workstation and Tool Design]

    M --> Q[ERGONOMIC SAFETY OUTCOME]
    N --> Q
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
```

**Figure 1:** Functional architecture showing how kinematic and kinetic outputs feed the major ergonomic safety tools (NIOSH, OWAS, REBA, RULA) used in industry.

## 4.2 Force/Torque Flow Diagram — Static Lifting Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph External[External Loads]
        A1[Box Weight: Wbox]
        A2[Trunk Weight: WHAT]
    end

    subgraph Internal[Internal Body Response]
        B1[Erector Spinae Force: FES at 5 cm]
        B2[Disc Compression: Fdisc at L5 S1]
    end

    subgraph Pivot[Pivot Point]
        C1[L5 S1 Lumbar Disc]
    end

    A1 -- Moment arm 40 cm --> C1
    A2 -- Moment arm 25 cm --> C1
    C1 -- Required counter-torque --> B1
    B1 -- Acts downward on disc --> B2
    B2 -- Compression load --> D[(Result: Compressive Force on Spine)]
    D --> E[Compare with NIOSH 3400 N Action Limit]
```

**Figure 2:** Force–torque flow in a two-handed sagittal lift. The erector spinae force, although small in displacement, generates a massive compressive force on the L5/S1 disc because of its small moment arm relative to the external loads.

## 4.3 Conceptual Flow — Muscle Force-Velocity Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Muscle Activation Neural Signal] --> B[Contractile Element Active Force]
    C[External Load on Muscle] --> D[Elastic Element Passive Force]
    B --> E[Total Muscle Force F]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Muscle Shortening Velocity v}
    F -->|v = 0| G[Isometric Maximum Force F0]
    F -->|v increases| H[Force Decreases hyperbolically]
    F -->|v = vmax| I[Zero Force Unloaded Shortening]
    H --> J[Power Output P = F x v]
    G --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Maximum Power at intermediate v]
```

**Figure 3:** Schematic of the Hill force-velocity relationship in skeletal muscle. Maximum power is delivered at intermediate contraction velocities, not at isometric (v=0) or at maximum unloaded velocity.

## 4.4 Joint Lever System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ThirdClassLever[Third Class Lever: Human Forearm]
        F1[Elbow Joint Fulcrum] -- 5 cm --> M1[Biceps Effort Force]
        M1 -- 30 cm --> L1[Load Weight in Hand]
    end

    F1 --> R1[Elbow Reaction Force FE]
    M1 --> R1

    L1 --> T1[Mechanical Disadvantage MA = 1 7]
    T1 --> U1[Speed and Range Advantage for Hand]
    T1 --> V1[High Internal Muscle Force Risk of Fatigue]
```

**Figure 4:** The biceps-brachii forearm lever system — a third-class lever with mechanical disadvantage of ~7:1, but with corresponding 7:1 speed advantage at the hand.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading tip for KTU students:** All four diagrams are interconnected. The muscle force-velocity relationship (Figure 3) determines the *maximum possible* $F_{ES}$ in the lifting analysis (Figure 2), which in turn is constrained by the lever geometry of the trunk and limbs (Figure 4), all within the analytical framework of Figure 1.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Define biomechanics. Distinguish between kinematics and kinetics with one human-body example for each. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

* **Biomechanics (1 mark):** The scientific study of the structure and function of biological systems, especially the human body, using the principles of mechanics.
* **Kinematics (1 mark):** The branch of biomechanics that describes motion (displacement, velocity, acceleration) without considering its causes. *Example:* Measuring the swing angle of a worker's arm during hammering.
* **Kinetics (1 mark):** The branch of biomechanics that analyzes the forces and moments that cause or result from motion. *Example:* Computing the compressive force on the L5/S1 lumbar disc during a manual lift.

### Question 2. State the Hill force-velocity relationship for skeletal muscle and explain its ergonomic significance. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

* **Hill's Equation (1 mark):**
  $$(F + a)(v + b) = (F_0 + a)b$$
  where $F$ is muscle force, $v$ is shortening velocity, $F_0$ is maximum isometric force, and $a$, $b$ are muscle-specific constants.
* **Relationship (1 mark):** The faster a muscle shortens, the less force it can generate; the slower (or isometric) the contraction, the more force it can produce. Force is maximum at $v = 0$ and zero at maximum shortening velocity.
* **Ergonomic Significance (1 mark):** Tasks requiring high force must be performed slowly (e.g., heavy lifting, pushing). Rapid motions with hand tools reduce the muscle's force capacity, increasing the risk of overuse injuries like tendinitis and carpal tunnel syndrome. This principle guides the design of tool handles, work rates, and job cycles.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, every Part B (14-mark) question in this module is an **Internal Choice**. Both alternatives are provided below. Each is divided into Part (a) for 7 marks and Part (b) for 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** Biceps-Brachii Lever and Static Lifting

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a) A worker holds a 6 kg load in the hand with the forearm horizontal. The distance from the elbow joint to the palm is 36 cm. The biceps tendon inserts on the radius at 4 cm from the elbow. The forearm + hand has a mass of 2.5 kg with its center of gravity at 16 cm from the elbow. Determine: (i) the force in the biceps tendon, (ii) the reaction force at the elbow joint. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Identify the forces and their points of application:*

Let $F_B$ = biceps force (upward) at 4 cm from elbow, $F_E$ = elbow reaction (assume upward), $W_L$ = load weight (downward) at 36 cm, $W_A$ = arm weight (downward) at 16 cm.

*Step 2 — Compute the weights:*

$$W_L = 6 \times 9.81 = 58.86 \text{ N}$$

$$W_A = 2.5 \times 9.81 = 24.525 \text{ N}$$

*Step 3 — Take moments about the elbow to eliminate $F_E$:*

$$F_B \times 0.04 = W_A \times 0.16 + W_L \times 0.36$$

$$F_B \times 0.04 = (24.525 \times 0.16) + (58.86 \times 0.36)$$

$$F_B \times 0.04 = 3.924 + 21.1896 = 25.1136$$

$$F_B = \frac{25.1136}{0.04} = 627.84 \text{ N}$$

*Step 4 — Apply vertical force equilibrium:*

$$F_E = F_B - W_A - W_L$$

$$F_E = 627.84 - 24.525 - 58.86 = 544.455 \text{ N}$$

**(i) Biceps force** = **627.84 N** *(upward, by the muscle)*
**(ii) Elbow reaction force** = **544.455 N** *(upward, by the humerus on the ulna)*

**(b) A worker lifts a 30 kg box from the floor in a stooped posture. The horizontal distance from the lumbar L5/S1 disc to the box center is 45 cm, and to the trunk's CG is 28 cm. The mass of the head, arms, and trunk (HAT) is 45 kg. The erector spinae muscle acts at a perpendicular distance of 5 cm from the L5/S1 disc. Determine: (i) the erector spinae muscle force, (ii) the compressive force on the L5/S1 disc. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Compute the relevant weights:*

$$W_{box} = 30 \times 9.81 = 294.3 \text{ N}$$

$$W_{HAT} = 45 \times 9.81 = 441.45 \text{ N}$$

*Step 2 — Apply moment equilibrium about L5/S1:*

$$F_{ES} \times 0.05 = W_{box} \times 0.45 + W_{HAT} \times 0.28$$

$$F_{ES} \times 0.05 = (294.3 \times 0.45) + (441.45 \times 0.28)$$

$$F_{ES} \times 0.05 = 132.435 + 123.606 = 256.041$$

$$F_{ES} = \frac{256.041}{0.05} = 5120.82 \text{ N}$$

*Step 3 — Apply vertical force equilibrium to find disc compression:*

The erector spinae muscle, when pulling the trunk upward, also compresses the disc (because it lies posterior to the disc and pulls the trunk backward, the reaction at the disc is forward and downward on the vertebra above):

$$F_{disc} = W_{box} + W_{HAT} + F_{ES}$$

$$F_{disc} = 294.3 + 441.45 + 5120.82 = 5856.57 \text{ N}$$

*Step 4 — Safety comparison:*

The NIOSH Action Limit is 3400 N. Since $F_{disc} = 5856.57 \text{ N} \gg 3400 \text{ N}$, this lift is **highly hazardous** and the workstation must be redesigned (e.g., reduce reach, reduce load, or use a lifting aid).

**(i) Erector spinae force** = **5120.82 N**
**(ii) L5/S1 disc compression** = **5856.57 N** (exceeds NIOSH limit by ~72%)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** Gait Kinematics and Centripetal Force

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a) During walking, the hip joint angle of a person changes from 25° extension at heel-strike to 10° flexion at mid-stance, over a duration of 0.15 s, with uniform angular acceleration. If the thigh length is 45 cm, find: (i) the average angular velocity of the thigh, (ii) the angular acceleration, (iii) the linear velocity of the knee at the instant of mid-stance. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Convert angles to radians:*

$$\theta_1 = 25° = 25 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.4363 \text{ rad}$$

$$\theta_2 = 10° = 10 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.1745 \text{ rad}$$

*Step 2 — Compute the average angular velocity:*

$$\bar{\omega} = \frac{|\theta_2 - \theta_1|}{\Delta t} = \frac{|0.1745 - 0.4363|}{0.15} = \frac{0.2618}{0.15} = 1.745 \text{ rad/s}$$

*Step 3 — Apply the uniform-acceleration kinematic equation:*

Assume the motion starts from rest at heel-strike, $\omega_1 = 0$:

$$\theta_2 = \theta_1 + \omega_1 t + \frac{1}{2} \alpha t^2$$

$$0.1745 = 0.4363 + 0 + \frac{1}{2} \alpha (0.15)^2$$

$$-0.2618 = 0.01125 \, \alpha$$

$$\alpha = \frac{-0.2618}{0.01125} = -23.27 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

(Negative sign indicates the thigh is decelerating during extension.)

*Step 4 — Compute the linear velocity of the knee at mid-stance:*

The knee is at the distal end of the thigh, i.e., at $r = 0.45$ m from the hip. First find $\omega$ at mid-stance:

$$\omega_2 = \omega_1 + \alpha t = 0 + (-23.27)(0.15) = -3.490 \text{ rad/s}$$

Magnitude: $|\omega_2| = 3.490$ rad/s

$$v_{knee} = r \cdot |\omega_2| = 0.45 \times 3.490 = 1.571 \text{ m/s}$$

**(i) Average angular velocity** = **1.745 rad/s**
**(ii) Angular acceleration** = **−23.27 rad/s²** (deceleration)
**(iii) Linear velocity of knee** = **1.571 m/s**

**(b) A worker uses a 2.0 kg hand tool by swinging it in a circular arc of radius 50 cm. The tool makes 30 revolutions per minute (rpm). Find: (i) the angular velocity in rad/s, (ii) the centripetal force on the tool, (iii) the centripetal acceleration. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Convert rpm to rad/s:*

$$\omega = 30 \text{ rpm} = 30 \times \frac{2\pi}{60} \text{ rad/s} = \pi \text{ rad/s} \approx 3.1416 \text{ rad/s}$$

*Step 2 — Apply the centripetal force formula:*

$$F_c = m r \omega^2 = 2.0 \times 0.50 \times (3.1416)^2$$

$$F_c = 1.0 \times 9.8696 = 9.8696 \text{ N} \approx 9.87 \text{ N}$$

*Step 3 — Apply the centripetal acceleration formula:*

$$a_c = r \omega^2 = 0.50 \times (3.1416)^2 = 0.50 \times 9.8696 = 4.935 \text{ m/s}^2$$

*Step 4 — Safety implication:*

The centripetal acceleration is approximately **0.5 g** (half of gravitational acceleration). The wrist and forearm must absorb this every cycle, and combined with the inertial loading of the tool, this contributes to repetitive strain injuries. *Ergonomic recommendation: reduce tool mass, reduce swing speed, or use anti-vibration handles.*

**(i) Angular velocity** = **3.1416 rad/s**
**(ii) Centripetal force** = **9.87 N**
**(iii) Centripetal acceleration** = **4.935 m/s²** (≈ 0.5 g)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Sign convention error in lifting problems (loses ~2 marks):** The erector spinae force adds to the disc compression — students often forget this and compute $F_{disc} = F_{ES}$ alone, missing the $W_{box}$ and $W_{HAT}$ terms.
> 2. **Unit conversion in angle problems (loses ~1 mark):** KTU expects angles in **radians** for $\omega$ and $\alpha$ formulas. Mixing degrees and radians in the same equation is a frequent mistake.
> 3. **Moment-arm error in static lever problems (loses ~2 marks):** The moment arm must be the **perpendicular distance** from the pivot to the line of action of the force, not the segment length.
> 4. **Forgetting to include arm weight in biceps problem (loses ~1 mark):** The forearm has its own weight acting at its own CG; this must be added to the moment equation.
> 5. **Hill's equation constants confusion (loses ~1 mark):** $F_0$ is force at $v=0$ (isometric maximum), NOT force at maximum velocity. $v_{max}$ occurs when $F = 0$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Quick-Reference Checklist (High-Density Revision)

* ☐ **Biomechanics = Mechanics applied to the human body.** Always classify the problem as **statics** (still) or **dynamics** (moving) first.
* ☐ **Kinematics** describes *how* something moves; **kinetics** explains *why* it moves.
* ☐ **Two scalar families:** linear ($s, v, a$) and angular ($\theta, \omega, \alpha$). The bridge between them is $v = r\omega$ and $a_t = r\alpha$, $a_n = r\omega^2$.
* ☐ **Reference human body mass = 70 kg; g = 9.81 m/s².** Commit these to memory.
* ☐ **Third-class levers dominate the skeleton** — biceps, calf, neck — giving speed/displacement advantage at the cost of high internal muscle force.
* ☐ **Biceps mechanical disadvantage ≈ 7:1** in a typical adult forearm. A 50 N held load → ~350 N biceps force.
* ☐ **Erector spinae lever ratio is even worse (~10:1)**, producing disc compressions of **8–15× the lifted weight**.
* ☐ **NIOSH disc compression Action Limit = 3400 N.** Always compare any computed $F_{disc}$ to this benchmark.
* ☐ **Hill's equation:** $(F+a)(v+b) = (F_0+a)b$ — high force at low velocity, low force at high velocity. Power output $P = Fv$ is maximum at intermediate velocity.
* ☐ **Work, Energy, Power:** $W = Fd\cos\theta$ (J), $KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$ (J), $PE = mgh$ (J), $P = Fv = \tau\omega$ (W). Mechanical efficiency of human body ≈ **20–25%**.
* ☐ **Centripetal force** $F_c = mr\omega^2 = mv^2/r$ — increases **quadratically** with rotation speed.
* ☐ **Gait analysis** uses $\theta(t)$, $\omega(t)$, $\alpha(t)$ for major joints; the hip, knee, and ankle each have characteristic flexion-extension profiles during the stance and swing phases.
* ☐ **Force analysis procedure:** (1) draw FBD, (2) list forces + moment arms, (3) apply $\sum F = 0$ and $\sum \tau = 0$, (4) solve simultaneously.
* ☐ **Body segment masses and CG locations (Dempster)** are used for multi-link static and dynamic analyses; know the head + trunk = ~58% of body mass and is centered at the sacrum.
* ☐ **Sustained human mechanical power output** is limited to **~15–20 W** for an average worker over an 8-hour shift; exceeding this leads to fatigue and injury.
* ☐ **Always interpret your numerical answer in safety terms** — KTU examiners award full 14 marks only when the engineering interpretation is included (e.g., "this exceeds the NIOSH limit; the workstation must be redesigned").

### Final Mnemonic for the Formulas

> **"LeVeL A" — Linear vs Angular link:**
> *L*inear **v**elocity = *E*quals **L**ength × *A*ngular velocity → $v = r\omega$

> **"FACTS"** — Forces, Accelerations, Centripetal, Torque, Static equilibrium:
> *F* = *ma*, *A* = $\alpha r$, *C* = $m\omega^2 r$, *T* = $I\alpha$, *S*um = 0.

This concludes the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 notes on **Biomechanics of Motion** for **OEAGT831 – Human Engg and Safety**, Module 2.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Movements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAGT831 - HUMAN ENGG AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Movements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the discipline of **Human Engineering (Ergonomics)**, the term **"Types of Movements"** refers to the systematic classification of human physical motions performed during the execution of a work task. According to standard KTU 2024 scheme ergonomics terminology, body movements are categorized based on the **body part involved**, the **degree of muscular effort**, the **precision and control demanded by the task**, and the **kinematic range** of the motion. Understanding these classifications is essential for designing workplaces, tools, machinery, and control layouts that minimize fatigue, reduce the risk of Cumulative Trauma Disorders (CTDs), and optimize human productivity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Under Module 2, the topic "Types of Movements" must be studied in conjunction with **biomechanics, work physiology, and the design of operator controls/displays**. The classification of movements forms the theoretical foundation for ergonomic job design, time-and-motion study, and Occupational Health & Safety (OHS) interventions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **conductor directing an orchestra**. The human body is the orchestra, and each movement is a different "instrument" being played. A **gross movement** is like the booming sound of a drum (using big muscles like your back and legs), while a **fine/precision movement** is the delicate plucking of a violin string (using small muscles like those in your fingers and wrist). Now think of a typist: they perform **tapping movements** (each key press is a quick tap), **contact movements** (a single purposeful touch), and **adjustment movements** (gross repositioning of the hand between keys). The orchestra is most harmonious when the right movement is assigned to the right task — and that is precisely what an ergonomist designs for!

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Ergonomic Metrics for Movement Analysis:**
> - **Maximum Recommended Lifting Force:** 23 kg (NIOSH standard for repetitive tasks)
> - **Ideal Reach Envelope (Horizontal):** **610 mm** (close reach) and **760 mm** (normal reach)
> - **Optimal Vertical Work Surface Height:** **950–1100 mm** from floor for seated work
> - **Maximum Repetition Rate (Finger Tapping):** 5–6 Hz (Hertz) for sustained activity
> - **Force Exertion Threshold (Fine Posture):** **< 15% Maximum Voluntary Contraction (MVC)** to prevent fatigue

## 1.4 Fundamental Categories of Human Movements (Overview)

Human movements in ergonomics are broadly classified into **five principal categories**:

1. **Gross Movements (Trunk/Whole-Body Movements)**
2. **Adjustment Movements (Coarse Positioning Movements)**
3. **Tapping Movements (Repetitive Discrete Contacts)**
4. **Contact Movements (Single Precise Touch)**
5. **Precision/Fine Movements (Highly Controlled Small Muscle Activity)**

Each category will be examined in exhaustive detail in the sections that follow.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Movements

### 2.1.1 Gross Movements

**Definition:** Gross movements involve the displacement of the entire body or large segments of the body. They engage the **large muscle groups** of the trunk, shoulders, thighs, and back.

**Why it matters:** These movements are associated with high energy expenditure. The metabolic cost of gross movement is directly proportional to body mass lifted and the distance moved.

**Characteristics:**
- Engage **large muscle groups** (deltoids, latissimus dorsi, quadriceps)
- High **metabolic cost** (Oxygen consumption increases by 0.5–1.0 L/min)
- Used for tasks such as lifting, walking, pushing, pulling
- Associated with risk of **lower-back injuries** if improperly designed

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Workplace Application:** Gross movements are governed by the **NIOSH Lifting Equation**, which determines the Recommended Weight Limit (RWL) for manual lifting tasks.

---

### 2.1.2 Adjustment Movements

**Definition:** Adjustment movements are coarse, intermediate-scale movements performed when an operator needs to **gross-position** a control, tool, or body part to a target zone before finer control is applied.

**Why it matters:** These movements are the bridge between gross and fine motor control. The operator first "approximates" the position (adjustment) and then "hones in" (contact/precision).

**Characteristics:**
- Engage **medium muscle groups**
- Moderate speed and accuracy
- Used when the task requires gross placement followed by refinement
- Example: a driver moving their hand to the gear lever before precisely shifting

---

### 2.1.3 Tapping Movements

**Definition:** Tapping movements are **rapid, repetitive, discrete contacts** made against a surface or control. The operator's task is to **maximize the number of contacts per unit time** while maintaining a consistent stroke.

**Why it matters:** Tapping tests are used in ergonomic job analysis to **measure the maximum rate of work** a muscle group can sustain. They form the basis of **Predetermined Motion Time Systems (PMTS)** such as Methods-Time Measurement (MTM).

**Characteristics:**
- Maximum sustainable rate: **5–6 taps per second** for finger
- Maximum sustainable rate: **2–3 taps per second** for arm
- Engages **small muscle groups** when done by fingers
- Used in data entry, piano playing, telegraph keying, machine feeding

---

### 2.1.4 Contact Movements

**Definition:** A contact movement is a **single, purposeful touch** made between an object (or body part) and a target, with no requirement for speed — only a successful single contact.

**Why it matters:** Contact movements differ from tapping in that **speed is not the measure**; **accuracy and successful contact** are the criteria.

**Characteristics:**
- Slow, controlled motion
- Single contact event
- Example: pressing a "print" button on a machine, touching a touch-screen icon

---

### 2.1.5 Precision / Fine Movements

**Definition:** Precision movements are highly controlled motions performed by **small muscle groups**, requiring a high degree of **accuracy, coordination, and steadiness**. They typically occur with very small displacements (often **< 5 mm**).

**Why it matters:** Precision movements are essential in surgery, watchmaking, electronics assembly, and quality inspection. They are the first to deteriorate with **fatigue, vibration exposure, or improper task design**.

**Characteristics:**
- Engages **small intrinsic muscles** (e.g., lumbricals, interossei)
- Energy expenditure is **low** but **neuromuscular demand is high**
- Susceptible to **tremor, fatigue, and accuracy loss**
- Sustained precision work is associated with **Repetitive Strain Injury (RSI)**

---

## 2.2 Classification by Anatomical Plane of Motion

| Anatomical Plane | Type of Movement | Example |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Sagittal Plane** | Flexion / Extension | Bending the elbow |
| **Frontal (Coronal) Plane** | Abduction / Adduction | Moving the arm away from the body |
| **Transverse (Horizontal) Plane** | Rotation / Pronation / Supination | Turning the head left/right |
| **Multi-Plane (Combined)** | Circumduction | Shoulder circular motion |

---

## 2.3 Classification by Muscle Activity Type

| Activity Type | Muscle State | Joint Movement | Example |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Isotonic (Dynamic)** | Muscle changes length | Joint moves | Lifting a box |
| **Isometric (Static)** | Muscle length constant | No joint movement | Holding a heavy object still |
| **Isokinetic** | Constant velocity | Controlled joint motion | Resistance training machines |
| **Ballistic** | Rapid acceleration | Fast, jerky motion | Throwing a ball |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Expression | Units | Application |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 1 | **Metabolic Cost of Movement** | $E = F \cdot d \cdot n$ | kcal | Energy expenditure of repetitive work |
| 2 | **Recommended Weight Limit (NIOSH)** | $RWL = LC \cdot HM \cdot VM \cdot DM \cdot AM \cdot CM \cdot FM$ | kg | Safe manual lifting weight |
| 3 | **Lifting Constant (LC)** | $LC = 23 \text{ kg}$ | kg | NIOSH baseline |
| 4 | **Work Done in Lifting** | $W = m \cdot g \cdot h$ | Joules (J) | Energy for vertical displacement |
| 5 | **Angular Velocity of Joint** | $\omega = \dfrac{\theta}{t}$ | rad/s | Joint rotation rate |
| 6 | **Linear Velocity of Hand** | $v = r \cdot \omega$ | m/s | Hand speed during arm rotation |
| 7 | **Lever Arm Torque (Biomechanics)** | $\tau = F \cdot d$ | N·m | Joint torque calculation |
| 8 | **Maximum Voluntary Contraction (MVC)** | $F_{max} = \text{Peak Force Output}$ | N | Strength baseline |
| 9 | **Endurance Limit (Rohmert Curve)** | $t_{lim} = \dfrac{T}{100 - T} \cdot k$ | minutes | Static posture endurance time |
| 10 | **Repetition Rate Threshold** | $f_{max} \le 5{-}6 \text{ Hz}$ (finger) | Hz | Sustainable tapping frequency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Key:** $F$ = Force, $d$ = distance, $n$ = number of repetitions, $m$ = mass, $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $h$ = height lifted, $\theta$ = angular displacement, $t$ = time, $r$ = radius, $T$ = percentage of MVC, $k$ = empirical constant.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Industrial Engineering:** Time-and-motion study uses movement classification to set **standard times** for tasks.
- **Workplace Design:** Control panel layout (height, distance, size) is decided based on the type of movement required.
- **Robotics & Prosthetics:** Joint movement types (flexion, rotation) are mirrored in **biomechatronic design**.
- **Rehabilitation Engineering:** Classification of movements guides therapy for post-injury recovery.
- **Occupational Safety:** Identifying repetitive movements prevents **Work-Related Musculoskeletal Disorders (WRMSDs)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Recommended Weight Limit (NIOSH Lifting Equation)

The **NIOSH Lifting Equation** is the most widely used model in ergonomics for determining the safe weight that a worker can lift under specified conditions. We will derive the steps conceptually.

### Step 1: Identify the Lifting Task Parameters

Let the load be lifted with the following measured parameters:

$$HM = \text{Horizontal Multiplier}$$

$$VM = \text{Vertical Multiplier}$$

$$DM = \text{Distance Multiplier}$$

$$AM = \text{Asymmetry Multiplier}$$

$$CM = \text{Coupling Multiplier}$$

$$FM = \text{Frequency Multiplier}$$

### Step 2: Establish the Lifting Constant

The baseline load that a healthy worker can lift under ideal conditions is:

$$LC = 23 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 3: Compute Each Multiplier

Each multiplier is a **dimensionless ratio** between the actual measured condition and the ideal reference condition.

$$HM = \dfrac{25}{H}$$

where $H$ is the horizontal distance of hands from the body in cm.

$$VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert V - 75 \vert$$

where $V$ is the vertical height of the hands in cm.

$$DM = 0.82 + \dfrac{1.8}{D}$$

where $D$ is the vertical travel distance in cm.

$$AM = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot A$$

where $A$ is the asymmetry angle in degrees.

$$CM = \begin{cases} 1.00 & \text{good coupling} \\ 0.95 & \text{fair coupling} \\ 0.90 & \text{poor coupling} \end{cases}$$

$$FM = \text{function of frequency and duration (tabular)}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Recommended Weight Limit

$$RWL = LC \cdot HM \cdot VM \cdot DM \cdot AM \cdot CM \cdot FM$$

### Step 5: Calculate the Lifting Index (LI)

The **Lifting Index** is a measure of physical stress:

$$LI = \dfrac{\text{Actual Load Weight (AL)}}{RWL}$$

- $LI \le 1.0 \Rightarrow$ **Safe** (most workers can perform without increased risk)
- $LI > 1.0 \Rightarrow$ **Increased Risk** (administrative or engineering controls required)
- $LI > 3.0 \Rightarrow$ **Unacceptable Risk** (task redesign is mandatory)

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Joint Torque (Biomechanical Lever Arm Analysis)

Consider a worker lifting a load with the forearm horizontal. The biceps inserts on the radius at a distance $d_b = 5$ cm from the elbow joint. The load is held in the hand at a distance $d_L = 35$ cm from the elbow.

### Step 1: Identify Forces and Lever Arms

$$F_b = \text{Force exerted by biceps}$$

$$F_L = m \cdot g = \text{Load weight force}$$

### Step 2: Apply Static Equilibrium

For the forearm to be in static equilibrium, the sum of torques about the elbow must equal zero:

$$\sum \tau_{elbow} = 0$$

$$F_b \cdot d_b - F_L \cdot d_L = 0$$

### Step 3: Solve for Biceps Force

$$F_b = \dfrac{F_L \cdot d_L}{d_b}$$

### Step 4: Substitute a Numerical Example

Let $m = 10$ kg, then $F_L = 10 \cdot 9.81 = 98.1$ N.

$$F_b = \dfrac{98.1 \cdot 0.35}{0.05}$$

$$F_b = \dfrac{34.335}{0.05}$$

$$F_b = 686.7 \text{ N}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** The biceps must exert a force **~7 times** the load weight because the muscle's lever arm is much shorter than the load's lever arm. This is why **lifting heavy objects close to the body** (reducing $d_L$) is biomechanically far safer than lifting with arms extended.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Static Endurance (Rohmert's Curve)

For a static muscular contraction sustained at a percentage $T$ of the Maximum Voluntary Contraction (MVC), the endurance time $t_{lim}$ can be estimated as:

$$t_{lim} = \dfrac{T}{100 - T} \cdot k$$

where $k$ is an empirical constant, often taken as $k \approx 60$ for postural muscles.

**Worked Example:** A worker holds a tool using 40% of their MVC.

$$t_{lim} = \dfrac{40}{100 - 40} \cdot 60$$

$$t_{lim} = \dfrac{40}{60} \cdot 60$$

$$t_{lim} = 40 \text{ minutes}$$

This means the worker should not hold this contraction statically for more than ~40 minutes without rest.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Movement Types vs. Ergonomic Design Variables

| Movement Type | Muscle Group | Energy Cost | Accuracy Demand | Speed Demand | Fatigue Onset | Design Implication |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **Gross** | Large | High | Low | Variable | Moderate | Use mechanical aids, reduce distances |
| **Adjustment** | Medium | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | Provide clear visual guides |
| **Tapping** | Small | Low | Low | High | High (repetitive) | Limit repetition rate, use ergonomic keyboards |
| **Contact** | Small | Low | High | Low | Low | Ensure adequate target size (Fitts' Law) |
| **Precision** | Small | Low | Very High | Low–Moderate | Very High | Tool balance, anti-vibration mounts, magnifiers |

---

## 3.5 Application of Fitts' Law to Movement Design

Fitts' Law relates movement time (MT) to the **distance** moved and the **size** of the target:

$$MT = a + b \cdot \log_2 \left( \dfrac{D}{W} + 1 \right)$$

where:
- $D$ = distance to the target
- $W$ = width (size) of the target
- $a, b$ = empirical constants (device-dependent)
- The term $\log_2 \left( \dfrac{D}{W} + 1 \right)$ is called the **Index of Difficulty (ID)**, measured in **bits**.

**Design Rule:** For precision movements, make targets **larger** and place them **closer** to the operator.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
