**(a)** Derive Fitts' Law for human motor performance from information-theoretic principles. Clearly define all variables and state the units. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A worker on a packaging line must reach **$A = 30$ cm** to grasp a component from a circular bin opening of **diameter $W = 3$ cm**. For this task, empirical constants are $a = 0.08$ s and $b = 0.15$ s/bit.  
    **(i)** Calculate the Index of Difficulty.  
    **(ii)** Calculate the predicted Movement Time.  
    **(iii)** Calculate the Index of Performance.  
    **(iv)** If the company redesigns the bin to a larger **$W = 6$ cm**, what is the **percentage reduction** in movement time? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
- (2 marks) Frame the motor act as a **communication problem**: the operator must select a final hand position from a continuous range. The target defines a *signal* (the acceptable width $W$); the neuromuscular system acts as a *channel* with inherent noise (motor variability).
- (2 marks) The number of **distinguishable final positions** within a $2A$ travel is $N = 2A/W$. By Shannon's information theory, the information content (in bits) needed to specify one of $N$ equiprobable positions is $H = \log_2(N) = \log_2(2A/W)$. This quantity is defined as the **Index of Difficulty (ID)**, measured in **bits**.
- (2 marks) Assuming the motor system transmits information at a **constant rate** (Index of Performance $IP = 1/b$ bits/s), the **Movement Time** is the time to transmit $ID$ bits plus an irreducible initialization time $a$:
$$MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{2A}{W}\right)$$
- (1 mark) This is **Fitts' Law** (1954). The constants $a$ (s) and $b$ (s/bit) are determined empirically via linear regression of experimental $MT$–$ID$ data.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Index of Difficulty:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
ID_1 &= \log_2\!\left(\frac{2 \times 30}{3}\right) = \log_2(20) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\ln 20}{\ln 2} = \frac{2.9957}{0.6931} \approx 4.32 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Predicted Movement Time:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
MT_1 &= 0.08 + 0.15 \times 4.32 \\[4pt]
&= 0.08 + 0.648 \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.728 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Index of Performance:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
IP &= \frac{1}{b} = \frac{1}{0.15} \approx 6.67 \text{ bits/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Redesign with $W = 6$ cm:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
ID_2 &= \log_2\!\left(\frac{2 \times 30}{6}\right) = \log_2(10) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\ln 10}{\ln 2} = \frac{2.3026}{0.6931} \approx 3.32 \text{ bits} \\[6pt]
MT_2 &= 0.08 + 0.15 \times 3.32 \\[4pt]
&= 0.08 + 0.498 = 0.578 \text{ s} \\[6pt]
\% \text{ Reduction} &= \frac{MT_1 - MT_2}{MT_1} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.728 - 0.578}{0.728} \times 100 \approx 20.6\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Doubling the target width from 3 cm to 6 cm **reduces movement time by ~20.6%** at the same accuracy level — a direct quantitative justification for ergonomic redesign of the bin.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]

**(a)** Explain the **Speed–Accuracy Trade-Off** in human performance. With a suitable diagram, describe the **Speed–Accuracy Operating Characteristic (SAOC)** and discuss how *practice* affects it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An aircraft cockpit designer must place two critical switches.  
    Switch X: $A = 25$ cm, $W = 1$ cm.  
    Switch Y: $A = 10$ cm, $W = 2$ cm.  
    Given $a = 0.05$ s and $b = 0.10$ s/bit, compute and compare the **Index of Difficulty**, **Movement Time**, and **Index of Performance** for both switches. Recommend which placement is more ergonomic and justify using Fitts' Law. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Speed–Accuracy Trade-Off & SAOC (7 marks):**
- (2 marks) The **Speed–Accuracy Trade-Off** states that for any given motor task, an operator cannot arbitrarily increase the speed of execution *and* maintain the same level of accuracy. Increasing movement speed shortens the time available for **sensory feedback correction**, raising endpoint variability and error rate.
- (2 marks) The **SAOC** is a plot of **Speed (operations/time)** on the x-axis and **Accuracy (% correct)** on the y-axis, with each point on the curve representing a feasible operating point for the same task. Skilled operators operate near the *knee* of the SAOC.
- (2 marks) **Practice** shifts the SAOC **outward (up and to the right)**: at the *same* speed, the operator achieves *higher* accuracy; at the *same* accuracy, the operator moves *faster*. The rate of improvement follows the **Power Law of Practice**: $MT_n = MT_1 \cdot n^{-\alpha}$, with $\alpha \approx 0.2$–$0.6$.
- (1 mark) Practical implication: training programs should aim to *reshape* the SAOC, not just push operators to work faster on the existing curve.

**(b) Numerical Comparison (7 marks):**

**Switch X ($A = 25$ cm, $W = 1$ cm):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
ID_X &= \log_2\!\left(\frac{2 \times 25}{1}\right) = \log_2(50) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\ln 50}{\ln 2} = \frac{3.912}{0.6931} \approx 5.64 \text{ bits} \\[6pt]
MT_X &= 0.05 + 0.10 \times 5.64 = 0.05 + 0.564 = 0.614 \text{ s} \\[4pt]
IP &= 1/b = 10 \text{ bits/s (same for both)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Switch Y ($A = 10$ cm, $W = 2$ cm):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
ID_Y &= \log_2\!\left(\frac{2 \times 10}{2}\right) = \log_2(10) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\ln 10}{\ln 2} \approx 3.32 \text{ bits} \\[6pt]
MT_Y &= 0.05 + 0.10 \times 3.32 = 0.05 + 0.332 = 0.382 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison and Recommendation:**

| Parameter | Switch X | Switch Y |
|---|---|---|
| $A$ (cm) | 25 | 10 |
| $W$ (cm) | 1 | 2 |
| $ID$ (bits) | 5.64 | 3.32 |
| $MT$ (s) | 0.614 | 0.382 |
| $IP$ (bits/s) | 10 | 10 |

- (1 mark) **Switch Y** is more ergonomic: it requires **~38% less movement time** (0.382 s vs. 0.614 s) at the same operator skill level because it has a **lower Index of Difficulty**.
- (1 mark) Recommendation: **redesign Switch X** by either reducing $A$ (moving it closer) or enlarging $W$ (making the button larger), especially if it is a frequently used or **safety-critical** control. A common cockpit guideline is to make **emergency switches at least 1.5–2× larger** than normal switches.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in $2A/W$. Many students write $ID = \log_2(A/W)$ and lose 1–2 marks. The factor 2 is essential (Shannon's correction for movement in either direction from origin).
> 2. **Mixing up $a$ and $b$.** $a$ is in **seconds** (intercept), $b$ is in **seconds/bit** (slope). $1/b$ is the Index of Performance.
> 3. **Unit errors in $ID$.** The Index of Difficulty is a *dimensionless* quantity expressed in **bits** — not in cm, seconds, or Hz.
> 4. **Forgetting the log base.** Fitts' Law uses $\log_2$ (bits), not $\log_{10}$ or $\ln$. Conversion factor: $\log_2(x) = \ln(x)/\ln(2)$.
> 5. **Ignoring the Power Law of Practice** in SAOC discussions. Examiners expect mention of *learning curves* in the speed–accuracy context.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Fitts' Law** is the **core equation** of the Speed–Accuracy trade-off: $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2(2A/W)$.
- [x] **$A$** = Amplitude (distance to target); **$W$** = Target width (tolerance).
- [x] **Index of Difficulty ($ID$)** is in **bits**; uses **$\log_2$**; always has the factor **2** in the numerator.
- [x] **Index of Performance ($IP$)** = $1/b$, in **bits per second**; measures operator skill.
- [x] The constants $a$ and $b$ are **empirical**, not theoretical — they depend on the limb, the device, and the user.
- [x] The **Speed–Accuracy Operating Characteristic (SAOC)** shifts outward with **practice** (Power Law of Practice).
- [x] **Hick's Law** ($RT = a' + b' \log_2 n$) is a *companion* law for *choice* reaction time (not movement time).
- [x] **Engineering implication:** To increase speed *without* losing accuracy, **enlarge $W$** (target size) or **reduce $A$** (distance to target).
- [x] **Effective width $W_e = 4\sigma_x$** is used in the modern **Shannon–MacFarlane** formulation for better empirical fit.
- [x] **Effective human motor throughput** is typically **4–12 bits/s** depending on the task and operator expertise.
- [x] Fitts' Law is a **mandatory KTU Module 2 topic** — expect at least one Part A (3 marks) and a possible Part B sub-part.
- [x] Always **state units** in derivations and solutions — the examiner is strict on dimensional and unit clarity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Human Control of Systems. Human Motor Activities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAGT831 - HUMAN ENGG AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Human Control of Systems. Human Motor Activities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Human Control of Systems — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Human Control of Systems** is the branch of Human Engineering (Ergonomics) that studies the human operator as an active, decision-making **controller element** within a man-machine system. It treats the human as a biological servo-mechanism that receives sensory information, processes it cognitively, and generates motor responses to regulate a machine or process toward a desired goal.

The human operator in a control loop is modeled as a **closed-loop feedback controller**, similar in principle to an engineering compensator but with inherent biological limitations such as:
- Variable reaction time (typically **200–250 ms** for simple stimuli)
- Learning curves and fatigue
- Limited bandwidth (approx. **1–2 Hz** for tracking tasks)
- Adaptive gain characteristics

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Note (Module 2):** This topic forms the analytical bridge between human sensory capabilities (covered earlier) and workplace design for manual tasks. It is a high-weightage topic in KTU ESE questions.

#### Conceptual Analogy
Think of **driving a car on a winding mountain road**:
- **Eyes (Display)** = The speedometer, road curvature, and lane markings → this is the *display/sensory input*
- **Brain (Controller)** = Processes: "Curve ahead, slow down, turn wheel 15°"
- **Hands/Feet (Actuator/Muscle)** = Executes the brake pedal and steering input
- **Car (Controlled Element)** = The vehicle dynamics
- **Feedback** = You constantly *see* the car's response and correct again

You are literally a **closed-loop controller** — and the smoother you drive, the better your "human transfer function" is tuned. In industrial settings, this exact human controller is being replaced by automation; when it cannot be replaced, it must be *designed for*.

### 1.2 Human Motor Activities — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Human Motor Activities** are voluntary, goal-directed physical movements produced by the coordinated contraction of skeletal muscles under central nervous system (CNS) control. In Human Engineering, motor activities are studied to design tasks, tools, and workstations that match human biomechanical capabilities, strength limits, and movement coordination.

Motor activities are classified by:
- **Precision required** (gross vs. fine motor skills)
- **Continuity** (discrete vs. continuous)
- **Sequence** (single vs. serial movements)
- **Feedback dependence** (open-loop/ballistic vs. closed-loop)

#### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine **threading a needle**:
- Gross motor moves your hand *toward* the needle (open-loop, fast)
- Fine motor makes tiny adjustments at the *tip* of the needle (closed-loop, slow, vision-guided)
The shift from gross → fine is the hallmark of skilled motor control, and workstation design must support *both* phases.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry:** Most workplace musculoskeletal disorders (MSDs), Cumulative Trauma Disorders (CTDs), and Repetitive Strain Injuries (RSIs) originate from poorly designed motor-activity demands — e.g., excessive force, awkward postures, or speed-accuracy mismatches.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Human as a Control System — Theoretical Framework

A human-controlled system contains the following functional blocks, mirroring a classical control loop:

1. **Input (Command / Desired State)** — Operator's intention, e.g., a target on a radar screen.
2. **Display (Sensory Input)** — Converts machine state into perceivable signals (visual, auditory, haptic).
3. **Human Controller** — Performs:
   - **Perception** (sensory registration)
   - **Cognition** (decision, prediction)
   - **Response Selection** (motor planning)
4. **Controlled Element (Machine/Process)** — The plant dynamics, e.g., a vehicle, robot, or chemical reactor.
5. **Feedback Path** — Sensory return (visual, tactile) closing the loop.

#### Classification of Human Control Tasks (KTU High-Yield)

| Task Type | Description | Example | Skill Demand |
|-----------|-------------|---------|--------------|
| **Compensatory** | Operator corrects only the *error* between display and reference. Display shows error only. | Driving a car keeping a lane marker centered | High — requires continuous mental computation |
| **Pursuit (Tracking)** | Display shows target *and* current output separately. Operator must match them. | Following a radar blip on a screen | Moderate — visual comparison |
| **Precognitive** | Operator pre-plans based on highly predictable input patterns. | Skilled typist, airline pilot on final approach | Lowest real-time load — relies on training |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Compensatory tasks are the *most* demanding on the human controller. The display design directly dictates the cognitive load. KTU questions frequently ask to *compare compensatory vs. pursuit tracking*.

### 2.2 Human Transfer Function Models

Modern Human Engineering models the human as a **linear quasi-static controller** with a pure time delay. The two classical models are:

#### 2.2.1 The Crossover Model (McRuer & Krendel, 1959 — publicly cited result)

For compensatory tracking tasks, the human-plus-neuromuscular system exhibits a **crossover frequency** behavior:

$$Y_h(s) = \frac{K \cdot e^{-\tau s}}{T_N s + 1}$$

Where:
- $K$ = human controller gain
- $\tau$ = effective reaction/transport time delay
- $T_N$ = neuromuscular lag time constant
- $e^{-\tau s}$ = pure time delay (Pade approximation often used)

The **crossover frequency** $\omega_c$ (rad/s) is the frequency at which the open-loop gain equals 1. For the human to remain stable and accurate, the system must be designed so that:

$$\omega_c \cdot \tau \approx 0.15 \text{ to } 0.35$$

If $\omega_c \cdot \tau$ exceeds ~0.5, the human can no longer control the system — instability and oscillation occur.

#### 2.2.2 The Optimal Control Model (Kleinman/Baron)

Treats the human as an **adaptive Kalman filter + optimal regulator**, with internal models of the plant. The operator continuously updates an internal model as task conditions change.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The crossover model is used in **aircraft handling-quality specifications** (e.g., MIL-STD-1797), **nuclear reactor control room design**, and **driver-vehicle modeling** for autonomous-vehicle safety validation.

### 2.3 Human Motor Activities — Theoretical Framework

#### 2.3.1 Classification of Motor Skills

| Dimension | Class 1 | Class 2 | Example |
|-----------|---------|---------|---------|
| Muscle Group | **Gross motor** (large muscles) | **Fine motor** (small muscles) | Lifting box vs. soldering |
| Continuity | **Discrete** (start–stop) | **Continuous** (cyclic) | Pressing a button vs. steering |
| Sequence | **Single** | **Serial** (ordered steps) | One press vs. typing a word |
| Feedback | **Open-loop (ballistic)** | **Closed-loop (visually guided)** | Punch vs. threading needle |

#### 2.3.2 The Open-Loop / Closed-Loop Dichotomy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adams' Closed-Loop Theory (1971)** states that motor learning involves two memory stores:
> 1. **Memory Trace** — built during practice (the "perceptual blueprint")
> 2. **Perceptual Trace** — built from feedback during action
> Skilled performance = Memory trace guides the action; perceptual trace corrects errors *only at the end* of the movement (catch-up correction).

- **Fast movements (< 200 ms)** → cannot use feedback → **open-loop / ballistic**
- **Slow movements (> 200 ms)** → can use ongoing feedback → **closed-loop**

#### 2.3.3 Stages of Motor Learning (Fitts & Posner)

| Stage | Cognitive Load | Movement Quality | Time to Reach |
|-------|---------------|------------------|---------------|
| **Cognitive** | Very high | Hesitant, many errors | Initial hours |
| **Associative** | Moderate | Errors reduced, refinement | Days–weeks |
| **Autonomous** | Low | Automatic, effortless | Months–years |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Summary Table)

| # | Concept | Formula / Relationship | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---------|------------------------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Fitts' Law** (Index of Difficulty) | $ID = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{2A}{W}\right)$ | $A$ = movement amplitude (m); $W$ = target width (m); $ID$ in **bits** | Speed-accuracy trade-off |
| 2 | **Fitts' Law** (Movement Time) | $MT = a + b \cdot ID$ | $a$ = start time (s); $b$ = slope (s/bit) | Empirical; $b$ is the **Index of Performance** reciprocal |
| 3 | **Hick's Law** (Choice Reaction Time) | $RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ | $n$ = number of choices; $RT$ in seconds | Information-theoretic |
| 4 | **Crossover Model Gain** | $Y_h(s) = \dfrac{K e^{-\tau s}}{T_N s + 1}$ | $K$ (gain); $\tau$ (s); $T_N$ (s) | Pure delay $e^{-\tau s}$ |
| 5 | **Stability Criterion** | $\omega_c \cdot \tau \approx 0.15 - 0.35$ | $\omega_c$ (rad/s); $\tau$ (s) | Beyond 0.5 → unstable |
| 6 | **Simple Reaction Time** | $RT \approx 0.20 \text{ to } 0.25$ s | — | Auditory faster than visual |
| 7 | **Single-Fitts Slope (typical)** | $b \approx 100 \text{ ms/bit}$ | — | Mouse pointing |
| 8 | **Channel Capacity (Motor)** | $C = \dfrac{ID_{max}}{MT_{min}} \approx 10 \text{ bits/s}$ | — | Human upper motor limit |
| 9 | **Force Exertion Limit (Steady)** | $F_{steady} \le 0.15 F_{max}$ | $F_{max}$ = MVC (Maximum Voluntary Contraction) | For sustained work |
| 10 | **Energy Expenditure (Light Work)** | $E \approx 5 \text{ kcal/min}$ | — | Industrial baseline |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical pipe rule:** All absolute-value / condition bars in formulas use `\vert` rendering — never raw `|` — to keep KTU markdown tables syntactically intact.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Driver-in-the-loop modeling for ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems) calibrates warning timing using Fitts' Law and crossover criterion.
- **Avionics:** Pilot workload assessment uses Hick's Law to limit cockpit alerts (typically $n \le 5$ critical).
- **Rehabilitation Engineering:** Fitts' Law quantifies post-stroke motor recovery via target-touching tests.
- **Workstation Design:** Motor-skill classification guides tool handle diameter, button size, and assembly sequence design.
- **Sports Engineering:** Speed-accuracy trade-off optimizes bat, racquet, and grip designs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Stability Margin in a Human-Controlled Loop

**Problem Setup:**
A compensatory tracking task is controlled by a human operator. The plant (machine) has a transfer function $Y_p(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$, i.e., a pure integrator (e.g., position control of a cart). The human's open-loop transfer function is $Y_h(s) = \dfrac{K e^{-\tau s}}{T_N s + 1}$. Find the **maximum stable gain** $K_{max}$ and prove the crossover criterion.

**Step 1 — Form the Open-Loop Transfer Function**

$$Y_{OL}(s) = Y_h(s) \cdot Y_p(s) = \frac{K \, e^{-\tau s}}{(T_N s + 1) \, s}$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude Condition at Crossover Frequency**

At $\omega_c$, the open-loop magnitude is unity:

$$\vert Y_{OL}(j\omega_c) \vert = 1$$

Evaluate magnitudes:

$$\vert e^{-j\omega_c \tau} \vert = 1, \quad \vert T_N j\omega_c + 1 \vert = \sqrt{1 + T_N^2 \omega_c^2}, \quad \vert j\omega_c \vert = \omega_c$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{K}{\omega_c \, \sqrt{1 + T_N^2 \omega_c^2}} = 1$$

Solve for $K$:

$$K = \omega_c \, \sqrt{1 + T_N^2 \omega_c^2}$$

**Step 3 — Phase Condition for Stability**

The total phase lag of the open-loop system is:

$$\phi_{OL}(\omega) = -\omega \tau - \tan^{-1}(T_N \omega) - 90^{\circ}$$

For closed-loop stability with reasonable phase margin (typically $30^{\circ}$–$60^{\circ}$), the phase must remain above $-180^{\circ}$ at crossover:

$$\phi_{OL}(\omega_c) = -180^{\circ} + PM$$

Setting $PM = 30^{\circ}$:

$$-\omega_c \tau - \tan^{-1}(T_N \omega_c) - 90^{\circ} = -150^{\circ}$$

$$\omega_c \tau + \tan^{-1}(T_N \omega_c) = 60^{\circ}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Crossover Approximation**

Empirically, for skilled human operators in compensatory tasks, the neuromuscular lag $T_N$ is *adjusted* by the operator to satisfy:

$$\tan^{-1}(T_N \omega_c) \approx 60^{\circ} - \omega_c \tau$$

This *adaptation* is what the human does automatically — and it is why training improves control.

**Step 5 — Solve for the Stability Constraint**

If we approximate the human's adjustment so that phase contribution of $(T_N s + 1)$ is fixed near $60^{\circ}$, then:

$$\omega_c \tau \approx 0.15 \text{ to } 0.35$$

This is the **crossover-model stability criterion**. Beyond $\omega_c \tau > 0.5$, no amount of operator adjustment can stabilize the loop — the system is "pilot-induced oscillation" (PIO) prone.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** For a typical pilot reaction time $\tau = 0.25$ s and crossover $\omega_c = 1.0$ rad/s, $\omega_c \tau = 0.25$ — within the safe band. For a fatigued operator with $\tau = 0.5$ s, the safe crossover drops to ~0.7 rad/s, meaning slow systems only.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example: Fitts' Law for a CNC Control Button

**Problem:** A CNC operator must press a control button. The button is $W = 12$ mm wide and the operator's hand approaches from $A = 240$ mm away. Compute the **Index of Difficulty**, **predicted Movement Time** (use $a = 50$ ms, $b = 100$ ms/bit), and the **Index of Performance**.

**Step 1 — Compute Index of Difficulty**

$$ID = \log_2\!\left(\frac{2A}{W}\right) = \log_2\!\left(\frac{2 \times 240}{12}\right) = \log_2(40)$$

$$ID = \frac{\ln 40}{\ln 2} = \frac{3.6889}{0.6931} = 5.322 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Movement Time**

$$MT = a + b \cdot ID = 50 + 100 \times 5.322 = 50 + 532.2 = 582.2 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Index of Performance (Throughput)**

$$IP = \frac{ID}{MT} = \frac{5.322 \text{ bits}}{0.5822 \text{ s}} = 9.14 \text{ bits/s}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation for Design**

$$\text{Throughput} \approx 9 \text{ bits/s} \Rightarrow \text{Adequate, but the button is small.}$$

If $W$ is enlarged to 30 mm:

$$ID_{new} = \log_2\!\left(\frac{480}{30}\right) = \log_2(16) = 4.0 \text{ bits}$$

$$MT_{new} = 50 + 100 \times 4.0 = 450 \text{ ms}$$

$$IP_{new} = \frac{4.0}{0.450} = 8.89 \text{ bits/s}$$

**Conclusion:** Enlarging the button reduces ID, reduces movement time by ~22%, and only marginally changes throughput. **Design rule: make the target wider before increasing its prominence.**

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Fitts' Law vs. Hick's Law

| Property | Fitts' Law (Motor) | Hick's Law (Cognitive) |
|----------|-------------------|-------------------------|
| **Domain** | Movement execution | Decision-making |
| **Independent variable** | $A$ (distance) and $W$ (target width) | $n$ (number of choices) |
| **Dependent variable** | $MT$ (movement time) | $RT$ (reaction time) |
| **Trade-off** | Speed vs. accuracy | Speed vs. complexity |
| **Tunable by design** | Button size, tool reach | Number of menu options, labels |
| **Industrial guideline** | $ID \le 6$ bits for routine tasks | $n \le 5$ alert options |
| **Failure mode** | Missed target | Wrong choice / hesitation |

### 3.4 Derivation: Reaction Time Decomposition (Donders' Subtractive Method)

Reaction time is decomposed additively:

$$RT_{total} = RT_{stimulus} + RT_{cognition} + RT_{motor}$$

- **$RT_{stimulus}$**: Sensory transduction (eye/ear → cortex) ≈ 50–80 ms
- **$RT_{cognition}$**: Decision time (Hick's Law) ≈ $b \log_2(n+1)$
- **$RT_{motor}$**: Motor planning + execution ≈ 100–150 ms

For a simple reaction (one stimulus, one response):

$$RT_{simple} \approx 200 \text{–} 250 \text{ ms}$$

For a choice reaction (2 stimuli):

$$RT_{choice} = a + b \log_2(2+1) = a + b \times 1.585$$

If $a = 150$ ms, $b = 150$ ms:

$$RT_{choice} = 150 + 150 \times 1.585 = 150 + 237.8 = 387.8 \text{ ms}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The difference $RT_{choice} - RT_{simple} \approx 130$ ms is the *cognitive processing time* — what Fitts' Law cannot account for, and where Hick's Law dominates.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Human as a Closed-Loop Controller (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Desired State / Command] --> B[Display Unit]
    B --> C[Sensory Input: Vision Audition Haptics]
    C --> D[Human Controller: Perception Cognition Decision]
    D --> E[Neuromuscular System]
    E --> F[Musculoskeletal Actuator: Hands Feet Voice]
    F --> G[Controlled Element: Machine Vehicle Process]
    G --> H[Feedback: Visual Auditory Tactile]
    H --> C
    G --> B
```

### 4.2 Classification of Human Control Tasks

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[Human Control Tasks]
    R --> P1[Compensatory Tracking]
    R --> P2[Pursuit Tracking]
    R --> P3[Precognitive Control]
    P1 --> P1D[Display shows ERROR only]
    P2 --> P2D[Display shows TARGET and OUTPUT separately]
    P3 --> P3D[Highly practiced predictable input]
    P1D --> P1L[Highest cognitive load]
    P2D --> P2L[Moderate cognitive load]
    P3D --> P3L[Lowest real-time load]
```

### 4.3 Motor Activity Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M[Human Motor Activities]
    M --> G[Motor Skill Type]
    M --> C[Control Mode]
    M --> L[Learning Stage]
    G --> G1[Gross Motor: Large muscles]
    G --> G2[Fine Motor: Small muscles]
    C --> C1[Open Loop: Ballistic fast 200ms]
    C --> C2[Closed Loop: Visually guided slow]
    L --> L1[Cognitive: Many errors high load]
    L --> L2[Associative: Refinement]
    L --> L3[Autonomous: Automatic low load]
```

### 4.4 Human Transfer Function — Crossover Model Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Input Reference] --> S[Subtractor: e = r minus y]
    S --> D[Display]
    D --> H[Human Controller: K exp to the power of minus tau s divided by TN s plus 1]
    H --> N[Neuromuscular Output]
    N --> P[Plant: 1 over s for integrator]
    P --> Y[Output y of t]
    Y --> S
    Y --> FB[Feedback Path]
    FB --> S
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram note:** For complex physical sketches (e.g., biomechanical posture diagrams), the equivalent **functional architecture** above conveys the same engineering relationships to examiners, which is what KTU valuation rewards.

### 4.5 Speed-Accuracy Trade-off in Fitts' Law

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Increase Target Width W] --> B[ID decreases]
    B --> C[Movement Time MT decreases]
    A --> D[Accuracy improves]
    E[Increase Movement Amplitude A] --> F[ID increases]
    F --> G[MT increases]
    E --> H[Errors increase for fixed W]
```

### 4.6 Reaction Time Decomposition (Donders)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RT[Total Reaction Time]
    RT --> R1[Stimulus Detection: 50 to 80 ms]
    RT --> R2[Cognitive Decision: Hick Law dependent]
    RT --> R3[Motor Planning and Execution: 100 to 150 ms]
    R2 --> R2A[n choices]
    R2 --> R2B[log2 n plus 1 law]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define human motor activity. Differentiate between gross and fine motor skills with one industrial example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Human motor activity is a voluntary, goal-directed movement of the body produced by skeletal muscle contraction, coordinated by the central nervous system, and studied in ergonomics to match task demands to human biomechanical limits.
- **Gross motor skills (1 mark):** Involve large muscle groups producing powerful, low-precision movements. *Example:* Lifting a 20 kg crate in a warehouse, operating a forklift steering wheel.
- **Fine motor skills (1 mark):** Involve small muscle groups producing precise, low-force movements. *Example:* Soldering a PCB joint, microsurgery, threading a needle.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*State Fitts' Law. Explain the term "Index of Difficulty" with an example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Statement (1 mark):** Fitts' Law models the speed-accuracy trade-off in aimed movements, expressing movement time as a linear function of task difficulty.
- **ID definition (1 mark):** $ID = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{2A}{W}\right)$, where $A$ = movement amplitude and $W$ = target width; measured in **bits**.
- **Example (1 mark):** Moving a mouse cursor 200 mm to a 20-mm-wide button gives $ID = \log_2(20) = 4.32$ bits. A smaller button (10 mm) at the same distance doubles the difficulty.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the human as a closed-loop controller with a neat functional block diagram. Explain compensatory, pursuit, and precognitive control tasks with industrial examples.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the crossover-model stability criterion for a human-in-the-loop compensatory tracking system where the human transfer function is $Y_h(s) = \dfrac{K e^{-\tau s}}{T_N s + 1}$ and the plant is $Y_p(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$. State the numerical range of $\omega_c \tau$ for stable operation.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Human as a Closed-Loop Controller (7 marks):**
1. **Block diagram (2 marks):** Reference → Subtractor → Display → Human Controller (Perception + Cognition + Motor) → Plant → Output. Feedback loop returns to the subtractor. (See Section 4.1.)
2. **Compensatory tracking (2 marks):** Display shows only the *error* between desired and actual state. Operator must mentally compute corrective action continuously. *Example:* A driver maintaining lane position using a lane-departure display.
3. **Pursuit tracking (1.5 marks):** Display shows target and output as two separate indicators. Operator matches the output to the target. *Example:* Anti-aircraft gunner tracking a target on a radar PPI scope.
4. **Precognitive control (1.5 marks):** Highly practiced tasks where the input pattern is predictable; the operator pre-programs motor sequences. *Example:* Skilled CNC machinist executing a known G-code cycle.

**(b) Crossover Model Stability (7 marks):**
1. **Open-loop transfer function (1.5 marks):**
$$Y_{OL}(s) = \frac{K e^{-\tau s}}{s(T_N s + 1)}$$
2. **Magnitude condition at crossover (2 marks):** Set $\vert Y_{OL}(j\omega_c) \vert = 1$:
$$K = \omega_c \sqrt{1 + T_N^2 \omega_c^2}$$
3. **Phase condition (2 marks):** Total phase lag must exceed $-180^{\circ}$ with adequate margin:
$$-\omega_c \tau - \tan^{-1}(T_N \omega_c) - 90^{\circ} > -180^{\circ}$$
4. **Crossover criterion (1.5 marks):** Skilled operator *adapts* $T_N$ so that
$$\boxed{\omega_c \tau \approx 0.15 \text{ to } 0.35}$$
Beyond $\omega_c \tau > 0.5$, pilot-induced oscillation (PIO) is likely.

---

**Q3(B). [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Alternative]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the open-loop and closed-loop modes of human motor control. State the role of feedback in each mode, citing Adams' Closed-Loop Theory.

**(b) [7 marks]** An industrial control button is 15 mm wide and located 300 mm from the operator's resting hand position. Using Fitts' Law with constants $a = 60$ ms and $b = 110$ ms/bit, calculate (i) the Index of Difficulty, (ii) the Movement Time, and (iii) the Index of Performance. Comment on the design implications.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop Motor Control (7 marks):**
1. **Open-loop (ballistic) control (2 marks):** Movements shorter than ~200 ms cannot be corrected mid-flight. Pre-programmed by the cerebellum based on prior practice. *Example:* A tennis serve, a hammer strike.
2. **Closed-loop (visually guided) control (2 marks):** Movements longer than ~200 ms use ongoing sensory feedback. The CNS compares actual vs. intended and issues corrections. *Example:* Threading a needle, parking a car.
3. **Adams' Closed-Loop Theory (2 marks):** Two memory stores: *Memory Trace* (built by practice) and *Perceptual Trace* (built by feedback). Skilled action = memory trace *initiates*; perceptual trace *corrects only at end* (catch-up correction).
4. **Diagram (1 mark):** Reference → Motor Program → Muscles → Movement → Sensory Feedback → Comparator (re-injected). (Refer Section 4.3.)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**
1. **Given (1 mark):** $W = 15$ mm, $A = 300$ mm, $a = 60$ ms, $b = 110$ ms/bit.
2. **Index of Difficulty (2 marks):**
$$ID = \log_2\!\left(\frac{2A}{W}\right) = \log_2\!\left(\frac{600}{15}\right) = \log_2(40) = 5.322 \text{ bits}$$
3. **Movement Time (2 marks):**
$$MT = a + b \cdot ID = 60 + 110 \times 5.322 = 60 + 585.4 = 645.4 \text{ ms}$$
4. **Index of Performance (1 mark):**
$$IP = \frac{ID}{MT} = \frac{5.322}{0.6454} = 8.24 \text{ bits/s}$$
5. **Design implication (1 mark):** $ID = 5.3$ bits is moderate; near the upper comfort limit. **Recommendation:** Enlarge the button to 25 mm, dropping $ID$ to 4.6 bits and $MT$ to ~566 ms — a 12% throughput gain with no loss of function.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Failing to state units:** $ID$ is in **bits**, $MT$ in **seconds**, $IP$ in **bits/s** — examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark per missing unit.
> 2. **Confusing Hick's Law with Fitts' Law:** Hick's Law is for *choice* reaction time (decision); Fitts' Law is for *aimed* movement time (motor). Mixing them = 2-mark loss.
> 3. **Skipping the diagram:** A neat block diagram is worth 1–2 marks in any "explain the human as a controller" question. Always include one.
> 4. **Forgetting the crossover numerical range:** Writing only "$\omega_c \tau < 1$" instead of the actual band 0.15–0.35 loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Neglecting industrial examples:** KTU values application context — every definition should be backed by at least one workplace example.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **Human Control of Systems** = studying the operator as a feedback controller within a man-machine loop.
- ✅ **Three task types:** Compensatory (highest load, error-only display) → Pursuit (separate target/output) → Precognitive (pre-planned, lowest load).
- ✅ **Crossover Model** transfer function: $Y_h(s) = \dfrac{K e^{-\tau s}}{T_N s + 1}$ with stability band $\omega_c \tau \approx 0.15$–$0.35$.
- ✅ **Typical reaction time:** **200–250 ms** simple; rises logarithmically with number of choices (Hick's Law).
- ✅ **Fitts' Law:** $ID = \log_2(2A/W)$ in **bits**; $MT = a + b \cdot ID$; design rule — increase $W$ before increasing $A$.
- ✅ **Motor skills:** Gross (large muscles, low precision) vs. Fine (small muscles, high precision).
- ✅ **Movement modes:** Open-loop/ballistic (< 200 ms, no feedback) vs. Closed-loop (> 200 ms, visually guided).
- ✅ **Motor learning stages:** Cognitive → Associative → Autonomous (decreasing cognitive load with practice).
- ✅ **Adams' Closed-Loop Theory:** Memory Trace (pre-planned) + Perceptual Trace (corrects at end).
- ✅ **Index of Performance (IP):** ~10 bits/s is the human motor channel ceiling.
- ✅ **Design heuristics:** $ID \le 6$ bits for routine tasks; $n \le 5$ choices for cockpit/HMI alerts; steady-state force $\le 15\%$ MVC.
- ✅ **Engineering applications:** ADAS warning timing, pilot workload assessment, workstation button sizing, rehabilitation robotics, sports-equipment design.
- ✅ **Watch-out units:** bits (ID), ms (MT), bits/s (IP), s (RT/τ), rad/s (ωc).
- ✅ **Common formula misuse:** Never use raw $\vert x \vert$ in tables; use `\vert` for markdown integrity.
- ✅ **KTU signature line for derivations:** Always state the *magnitude condition* and *phase condition* explicitly in crossover-model problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Controls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEAGT831 - HUMAN ENGG AND SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Controls # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 What Are "Controls" in Human Engineering? In the discipline of **Human Engineering (Ergonomics)**, the term **Controls** refers to any physical device, mechanism, or interface element that a human operator manipulates to **initiate, modify, regulate, or terminate** the functioning of a machine, system, or process. Controls are the *actuator-side* of the famous **Human–Machine System (HMS)** triad, sitting between the operator's limbs (input) and the machine's functional components (output). > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition:** A control is a manually operated component through which the human operator transmits energy or information to a machine, translating human intent into machine action. Controls are the human-in-the-loop transducers of intent. ### The Human–Machine Closed Loop A control is never designed in isolation. It exists within the **Human–Control–Display–Machine** feedback loop: $$ \text{Human Operator} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Control} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Machine State} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Display} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Human Operator} $$ The control receives *input from the operator*, and the *display feeds back* information about what that input achieved. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Steering Wheel and the Faucet Imagine you are driving a car. You push the **accelerator pedal (control)** → the engine speeds up → the **speedometer (display)** shows the new speed. Simultaneously, you rotate the **steering wheel (control)** → the front wheels turn → you observe the **road (display)** changing. Every knob, lever, pedal, button, and switch you touch is a **control**. Now picture a **kitchen faucet tap**. You turn a **knob (control)** → water flows → you hear and feel the change. The knob's resistance, shape, and rotation angle have all been ergonomically designed so your wrist muscles, finger grip, and forearm can operate it comfortably. **This is exactly what Human Engineering Controls design is about — matching the human body's biomechanical capabilities (strength, reach, dexterity, range of motion) to the machine's input requirements.** --- ## 1.3 Why Are Controls a Critical Topic in Safety Engineering? According to KTU syllabus weighting, controls form one of the three pillars of human-machine interaction (alongside displays and the workplace). Poorly designed controls cause: - **Operator fatigue** and **musculoskeletal disorders (MSDs)** - **Accidental activation** leading to industrial accidents - **Slow response time** in emergencies (e.g., emergency stop buttons) - **Mode errors** where the wrong control is selected > [!IMPORTANT] > **Safety-Critical Note:** A significant percentage (~60–80%, as per various NTSB and HSE reports) of industrial and aviation accidents trace back to *control-design* failures rather than mechanical failure. The control is the human's only authorized doorway into the machine — design it wrong and you have built a hazard. --- ## 1.4 Fundamental Physical & Biomechanical Constants (Standard Metrics) Human Engineering uses several reference values drawn from anthropometric studies: | Constant / Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | |---|---|---| | Maximum fingertip push/pull force (seated, two-handed) | $F_{push,max}$ | $\approx \mathbf{230 \; N}$ | | Maximum hand grip force | $F_{grip}$ | $\approx \mathbf{300 \; N}$ | | Maximum foot push force (leg extended) | $F_{foot}$ | $\approx \mathbf{900 \; N}$ | | Standard resting wrist deviation angle | $\theta_{rest}$ | $\mathbf{0^\circ \text{ to } 15^\circ}$ | | Comfortable finger span for knob | $d_{span}$ | $\mathbf{25 \; mm \; to \; 75 \; mm}$ | > [!NOTE] > These figures are derived from population studies (5th–95th percentile range) and are tested standards in KTU-style numerical questions. --- ## 1.5 Classification Preview Controls are classified along **three independent axes**: 1. **By Mode of Operation** → Hand Controls vs Foot Controls 2. **By Control Function** → Discrete (binary) vs Continuous (proportional) 3. **By Movement Geometry** → Linear, Rotary, Push-Pull A full classification taxonomy is presented in **SECTION 4**. # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Operational Logic of a Control — Step-by-Step A control, regardless of its physical form, performs **five sequential ergonomic functions**. KTU examiners frequently test this layered model. 1. **Identification (Recognition)** - The operator must *recognize* which control to use. Achieved through **shape coding, color coding, labeling, and location grouping** (e.g., emergency stops are red mushroom-headed). 2. **Acquisition (Reach + Grasp)** - The hand/foot must physically reach and acquire the control. Determined by **anthropometric reach envelopes** and **grip clearance**. 3. **Manipulation (Activation)** - Force is applied to actuate the control. The required force must be **between the operator's minimum precision threshold and maximum strength threshold**. For most adults: $$ F_{min,precision} \approx 2 \text{ to } 5 \;\text{N}, \qquad F_{max,strength} \approx 230 \;\text{N} $$ 4. **Feedback Conformance (Lock-in/Detent)** - The control provides **tactile, auditory, or visual feedback** confirming actuation (e.g., a click, detent, or LED). This prevents *partial activation* errors. 5. **Release and Recoil** - Once released, the control returns to a **defined neutral state** (spring-returned). For discrete controls, this is mandatory for safety. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Pop-Up" Principle (KTU Favourite):** When a control is *deactivated*, it should visually and physically **return to the "off" position**. Push-buttons pop out, toggles return, levers spring back. This is the basis of fail-safe design and prevents dangerous "leftover" states. --- ## 2.2 Hand Controls — The Dominant Class ### 2.2.1 Knobs A **knob** is a rotary control rotated between the thumb and forefinger or gripped with the whole hand. **Design Logic:** - **Small knobs (≤ 25 mm diameter):** rotated between fingertips — fine, precise adjustment. - **Large knobs (≥ 75 mm diameter):** grasped with whole hand — coarse, high-torque adjustment. - **Knurled surfaces** enhance grip and prevent slippage when hands are oily or gloved. **Recommended Torque Range (KTU value):** $$ T_{knob} = 0.5 \;\text{N·m} \;\text{to}\; 2.5 \;\text{N·m} $$ ### 2.2.2 Cranks A **crank** is a rotating control with an offset handle used for repeated circular motion. Think of a hand-cranked winch. **Design Logic:** - Radius of rotation must accommodate the **full arm-swing arc**. - Crank length $L$ should keep the hand within the **functional reach zone**: $$ L_{optimal} \approx \frac{L_{forearm}}{4} \;\text{to}\; \frac{L_{forearm}}{2} $$ - Standard: $L \approx \mathbf{100 \text{ to} 175 \; mm}$. ### 2.2.3 Levers A **lever** is a rigid bar pivoted at one end, transmitting force by rotation. **Two sub-classes:** - **Vertical Lever (Joystick):** Multi-directional; used in aircraft, cranes, excavators. - **Horizontal Lever (Gear Shift, Brake):** Linear movement in a plane. **The Three Lever Movements (Cumulative Effect):** $$ \text{Total Displacement} = \sum_{i=1}^{3} \left( x_i \right) $$ The three canonical lever motions for maximum precision are **wrist flexion, wrist deviation, and forearm rotation**. ### 2.2.4 Push Buttons Discrete, binary control. Pressed once = on, released = off (spring-returned). **Critical Dimensions (Mushroom vs Flush):** - **Flush button** — recessed to prevent accidental activation. - **Mushroom button** — protrudes; used for **EMERGENCY STOP**. > [!WARNING] > Emergency stop buttons *must* be **red, mushroom-headed, palm-operated**, and must **latch mechanically** (stay pressed until manually reset). This is a non-negotiable safety standard (ISO 13850). ### 2.2.5 Wheels (Steering Wheels, Handwheels) A wheel is a large rotary control requiring both hands. Its mechanical advantage is given by: $$ \text{Mechanical Advantage (MA)} = \frac{R_{wheel}}{r_{hub}} $$ where $R_{wheel}$ is the wheel's outer radius and $r_{hub}$ is the hub radius (where force is applied). --- ## 2.3 Foot Controls — Pedals and Treadles Foot controls **free the hands** for other concurrent tasks. They are used when: - Force requirements are **high** (foot muscles are stronger). - Operation is **continuous and rhythmic** (driving). - Hands are **occupied** (pilot, machine operator). **Design Logic for Pedals:** - Pedal angle: $\theta_{pedal} \approx \mathbf{20^\circ \text{ to} 30^\circ}$ from vertical. - Pedal travel: $d_{travel} \approx \mathbf{25 \text{ to} 64 \; mm}$. - Maximum foot force (sustained): $\approx \mathbf{90 \; N}$. --- ## 2.4 Discrete vs Continuous Controls | Property | Discrete Control | Continuous Control | |---|---|---| | Function | Binary ON/OFF | Variable input | | Examples | Push button, toggle switch | Steering wheel, throttle lever, joystick | | Feedback | Tactile click, detent | Smooth proportional resistance | | Operator Training | Minimal | High — requires finesse | | Error Sensitivity | Mode errors, missed actuation | Calibration drift, overshoot | --- ## 2.5 Control–Display Compatibility (Sterling's Principles) When a control is paired with a display, their **spatial and movement relationships** must be intuitive. **Mary-Louise Gillern (Sterling)** established three laws: 1. **Spatial Compatibility:** The control moves in the *same direction* as the display indicator. (Turning a steering wheel clockwise should turn the indicator clockwise.) 2. **Movement Compatibility:** The control–display ratio should remain **constant** (linear relationship), not accelerate or jump. 3. **Cultural Stereotype:** Right = increase, Up = more, Forward = on (these have cultural inertia). **Mathematical Form of Compatibility:** $$ \text{Control–Display Ratio (C/D)} = \frac{\Delta \theta_{control}}{\Delta \theta_{display}} $$ For *ideal* compatibility, $\text{C/D} \approx \mathbf{1}$ (1:1 movement transfer). --- ## 2.6 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table summarizes **every formula** you will need for KTU ESE / internal assessments on this topic. Memorize these. | # | Formula / Constant | Meaning | Standard Range / Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $MA = \dfrac{R_{wheel}}{r_{hub}}$ | Mechanical Advantage of a wheel | Dimensionless | | 2 | $T = F \times r$ | Torque from knob/lever | $\text{N·m}$ | | 3 | $C/D = \dfrac{\Delta \theta_{control}}{\Delta \theta_{display}}$ | Control–Display ratio | Ideal = 1 | | 4 | $\theta_{wrist,rest} = 0^\circ$ to $15^\circ$ | Neutral wrist deviation | Degrees | | 5 | $F_{push,max} \approx 230 \;\text{N}$ | Max seated push force | Newtons | | 6 | $F_{foot,max} \approx 900 \;\text{N}$ | Max foot extension force | Newtons | | 7 | $T_{knob} = 0.5$ to $2.5 \;\text{N·m}$ | Knob torque range | N·m | | 8 | $L_{crank} \approx \dfrac{L_{forearm}}{4} \text{ to } \dfrac{L_{forearm}}{2}$ | Optimal crank length | mm | | 9 | $F_{precision,min} = 2$ to $5 \;\text{N}$ | Min force for fine control | Newtons | | 10 | $\theta_{pedal} = 20^\circ$ to $30^\circ$ | Pedal inclination | Degrees | | 11 | $d_{pedal} = 25$ to $64 \;\text{mm}$ | Pedal travel | mm | > [!NOTE] > **Anti-pipe rule observed:** All absolute value expressions (e.g., $\vert F \vert$) have been replaced with `\vert` or inlined text to preserve markdown table integrity. --- ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aviation:** Yoke (wheel), throttle (lever), rudder pedals (foot) — triple-redundant control hierarchy. - **Automotive:** Steering wheel, gear shift, pedals — C/D ratio ~17:1 (steering ratio). - **Industrial CNC:** Handwheels for jog-mode, push buttons for cycle start. - **Medical Surgery:** Foot pedals for electrocautery, hand switches for laser power. - **Heavy Machinery:** Joysticks for excavators with **isometric** (force-based) control for precision. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation I: Optimal Crank Length from Anthropometric Data **Problem Context (Standard KTU Question):** Given a population forearm length (elbow to fingertip) of $L_{forearm} = 460 \;\text{mm}$, derive the optimal crank handle length $L_{crank}$ for a hand-cranked winch, assuming the operator must be able to complete a full $360^\circ$ rotation without shoulder strain. ### Step 1 — Define the Biomechanical Constraint For a continuous hand-crank motion, the hand must travel along an arc. The center of this arc is the **elbow joint**, and its radius is the **forearm length**. The crank's handle must therefore be sized so that: $$ L_{crank} \leq L_{forearm} - L_{wrist,buffer} $$ where $L_{wrist,buffer}$ is a safety margin (~50 mm) to keep the wrist in its neutral zone. ### Step 2 — Apply the Quarter-to-Half Forearm Rule (Empirical) Standard ergonomics literature (Wickens, Sanders & McCormick) recommends: $$ L_{crank,optimal} = k \times L_{forearm}, \qquad k \in \left[\tfrac{1}{4}, \tfrac{1}{2}\right] $$ ### Step 3 — Substitute the Numerical Values Given $L_{forearm} = 460 \;\text{mm}$: $$ L_{crank,min} = \tfrac{1}{4} \times 460 = 115 \;\text{mm} $$ $$ L_{crank,max} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 460 = 230 \;\text{mm} $$ ### Step 4 — Apply the Safety Buffer and Practical Range Subtracting the wrist buffer of $50 \;\text{mm}$ from the upper bound (so the wrist remains free): $$ L_{crank,optimal} \in \left[115 \;\text{mm}, 180 \;\text{mm}\right] $$ ### Step 5 — Final Design Recommendation **Adopt $L_{crank} \approx \mathbf{150 \; mm}$** as the design value — a midpoint that: - Allows full $360^\circ$ rotation - Keeps wrist deviation below $15^\circ$ - Provides sufficient mechanical advantage for typical winch loads. --- ## 3.2 Derivation II: Mechanical Advantage of a Steering Wheel **Problem Context (Standard KTU Question):** A steering wheel has outer radius $R_{wheel} = 200 \;\text{mm}$ and hub radius $r_{hub} = 40 \;\text{mm}$. An operator applies tangential force $F_{hand} = 50 \;\text{N}$ at the outer rim. Calculate: (a) The mechanical advantage. (b) The torque transmitted to the steering column. (c) The output force at the wheel-to-column interface. ### Step (a) — Mechanical Advantage $$ MA = \frac{R_{wheel}}{r_{hub}} = \frac{200 \;\text{mm}}{40 \;\text{mm}} = 5 $$ The wheel provides a **5× force amplification**. ### Step (b) — Torque on the Steering Column Torque is the product of force and lever arm. Since the hand applies force at the outer rim: $$ T_{hand} = F_{hand} \times R_{wheel} = 50 \;\text{N} \times 0.200 \;\text{m} = 10 \;\text{N·m} $$ By conservation of energy (ignoring friction), the same torque is transmitted to the column: $$ T_{column} = T_{hand} = 10 \;\text{N·m} $$ ### Step (c) — Output Force at the Hub Interface This torque is delivered at the smaller hub radius: $$ F_{column} = \frac{T_{column}}{r_{hub}} = \frac{10 \;\text{N·m}}{0.040 \;\text{m}} = 250 \;\text{N} $$ ### Step 4 — Verification via Mechanical Advantage $$ F_{output} = MA \times F_{hand} = 5 \times 50 = 250 \;\text{N} \;\;\checkmark $$ The result is **consistent**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation III: Control–Display Ratio and Operator Error **Problem Context (Standard KTU Question):** A crane operator controls a horizontal boom via a joystick. Moving the joystick $10^\circ$ forward causes the boom to lift by $2.5^\circ$. Compute the Control–Display ratio and comment on compatibility. ### Step 1 — State the Definition $$ C/D = \frac{\Delta \theta_{control}}{\Delta \theta_{display}} $$ ### Step 2 — Substitute Values $$ C/D = \frac{10^\circ}{2.5^\circ} = 4 $$ ### Step 3 — Interpret the Result A **C/D ratio of 4** means the operator must move the control **4 times more** than the display moves. This is a **fine-tuning configuration** — desirable for precise positioning of heavy loads. ### Step 4 — Compare to the Standard Sterling's *ideal* ratio for maximum intuitive feel is $C/D \approx 1$. Ratios between **2 and 6** are acceptable for precision tasks; ratios $>10$ cause **training overhead and operator fatigue**. **Conclusion:** A C/D of 4 is **within the recommended ergonomic range** for crane operation. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Correct Control Type | Task Requirement | Recommended Control | Reason | |---|---|---| | Emergency stop | Mushroom-head palm button (red) | Latching, fail-safe, high-visibility | | Continuous speed variation | Lever or rotary knob | Smooth proportional output | | One-handed mode selection | Toggle switch | Clear visual on/off state | | High-force actuation | Foot pedal | Foot muscles ~4× stronger than fingers | | Precision fine-tuning | Small rotary knob | High mechanical resolution | | Multidirectional control (3D) | Joystick / isometric stick | Compact, degrees-of-freedom | This matrix is a **favourite KTU Part A question** — memorize it. # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Master Classification of Controls (Mermaid Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[CONTROLS in Human Engineering] --> B[By Mode of Operation] A --> C[By Function Type] A --> D[By Movement Geometry] B --> B1[Hand Controls] B --> B2[Foot Controls] B1 --> B11[Knobs] B1 --> B12[Cranks] B1 --> B13[Levers] B1 --> B14[Push Buttons] B1 --> B15[Wheels and Handwheels] B1 --> B16[Thumbwheels] B2 --> B21[Pedals] B2 --> B22[Treadles] B2 --> B23[Knee Controls] C --> C1[Discrete Binary] C --> C2[Continuous Proportional] C --> C3[Multiplexed Sequential] D --> D1[Linear Translation] D --> D2[Rotary Motion] D --> D3[Push-Pull along Axis] ``` > [!NOTE] > The flowchart above shows the **three-axis classification system** used in KTU reference material. Memorize the three axes: *mode*, *function*, and *geometry*. --- ## 4.2 Control–Display Feedback Loop (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR OP[Human Operator] -->|Force F| CTRL[CONTROL
Knob, Lever, Pedal] CTRL -->|Mechanical Linkage| MACH[MACHINE STATE
Speed, Position, Power] MACH -->|Sensor Signal| DISP[DISPLAY
Gauge, Screen, Indicator] DISP -->|Visual or Auditory Cue| OP OP -->|Corrected Force| CTRL style OP fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#000 style CTRL fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#000 style MACH fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#000 style DISP fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#000 ``` This closed-loop diagram illustrates the **continuous interaction cycle** that every ergonomic control must enable. --- ## 4.3 Knob Design Specification (Block-Level Functional Architecture) Since precise physical drawings of knob geometries are difficult to render in Mermaid, the following block diagram conveys the **functional specifications** instead. ```mermaid flowchart TD K[KNOB DESIGN SPECIFICATION] --> K1[GEOMETRY] K --> K2[SURFACE TEXTURE] K --> K3[ROTATIONAL RESISTANCE] K --> K4[FEEDBACK TYPE] K1 --> K1a[Diameter: 25 to 75 mm] K1 --> K1b[Height: 15 to 25 mm] K1 --> K1c[Shape: Cylindrical or D-shaped] K2 --> K2a[Knurled for high grip] K2 --> K2b[Smooth for low-friction] K2 --> K2c[Ribbed for tactile differentiation] K3 --> K3a[Continuous smooth resistance] K3 --> K3b[Detented stepped resistance] K3 --> K3c[Brake-loaded high resistance] K4 --> K4a[Visual: pointer or scale] K4 --> K4b[Tactile: detent clicks] K4 --> K4c[Auditory: click sounds] style K fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#000 ``` --- ## 4.4 Foot Pedal Design Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD P[FOOT PEDAL DESIGN] --> P1[Pedal Surface Area] P --> P2[Pedal Angle] P --> P3[Pedal Travel Distance] P --> P4[Resistance Type] P1 --> P1a[Minimum 60 mm by 80 mm] P2 --> P2a[20 to 30 degrees from vertical] P3 --> P3a[25 to 64 mm total stroke] P4 --> P4a[Spring-returned for safety] P4 --> P4b[Resistance proportional to load] style P fill:#fad7a0,stroke:#000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > These block diagrams substitute the *functional specification* of physical components because Mermaid cannot render realistic mechanical drawings. They are *equally valid* for KTU board exams and signal strong preparation. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q: Define "Control" in the context of Human Engineering. List any four types of hand controls with one example each.** **Model Answer:** A control is a manually operated device through which a human operator transmits energy or information to a machine, translating human intent into machine action. Four types of hand controls: 1. **Knob** — e.g., volume control on a radio. 2. **Lever** — e.g., gear-shift lever in a car. 3. **Push button** — e.g., elevator call button. 4. **Cranks** — e.g., hand-cranked winch in old jeeps. *(Any four with one example each — full 3 marks.)* --- ### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q: What is the "Pop-Up" principle in control design? Why is it critical for safety?** **Model Answer:** The Pop-Up principle states that **a control must physically return to its neutral "off" position once the operator releases it** (e.g., push-buttons pop out, spring-loaded levers snap back). It is critical for safety because: - It prevents **residual / leftover activation** that could cause unintended machine motion. - It enforces the **fail-safe default** — if the operator is incapacitated, the system automatically returns to safe state. - It is the basis of standards like **ISO 13850** for emergency stop design. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A [KTU University Exam – May 2024 — 14 Marks]** **Q: (a)** Explain in detail the classification of controls based on **mode of operation** and **function type**, with engineering examples for each category. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A steering wheel has outer radius $350 \;\text{mm}$ and hub radius $35 \;\text{mm}$. An operator applies a tangential force of $40 \;\text{N}$ at the outer rim. Compute the **mechanical advantage**, the **torque transmitted to the steering column**, and the **output force at the hub**. Comment on the ergonomic implications. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A** **Part (a) — Classification of Controls** Controls are classified along **three independent axes**, but the question asks for two: **(i) By Mode of Operation:** 1. **Hand Controls** — operated by fingers, palm, or whole hand. - Examples: knobs (radio tuning), levers (gear shift), push-buttons (calculator keys), wheels (steering wheel), cranks (hand winch), thumbwheels (mouse scroll). 2. **Foot Controls** — operated by foot or leg. - Examples: pedals (accelerator, brake), treadles (sewing machine), knee controls (some aircraft cockpit levers). **(ii) By Function Type:** 1. **Discrete (Binary) Controls** — only two states (ON/OFF). - Examples: toggle switches, push-buttons, emergency stop. 2. **Continuous (Proportional) Controls** — variable output proportional to operator input. - Examples: steering wheel, throttle lever, rotary dimmer, joystick. 3. **Multiplexed / Sequential Controls** — single control cycles through multiple states. - Examples: rotary selector with detents, multi-position toggle. *Conclusion: Each axis governs a different design decision. Mode of operation determines **biomechanical loading**; function type determines **information flow and feedback design**.* --- **Part (b) — Steering Wheel Numerical** **Given:** - $R_{wheel} = 350 \;\text{mm} = 0.350 \;\text{m}$ - $r_{hub} = 35 \;\text{mm} = 0.035 \;\text{m}$ - $F_{hand} = 40 \;\text{N}$ (applied tangentially at outer rim) **Step 1 — Mechanical Advantage:** $$ MA = \frac{R_{wheel}}{r_{hub}} = \frac{0.350}{0.035} = 10 $$ **Step 2 — Torque on the Steering Column:** $$ T = F_{hand} \times R_{wheel} = 40 \;\text{N} \times 0.350 \;\text{m} = 14 \;\text{N·m} $$ By conservation of torque (ideal rigid shaft), $T_{column} = 14 \;\text{N·m}$. **Step 3 — Output Force at the Hub:** $$ F_{column} = \frac{T_{column}}{r_{hub}} = \frac{14 \;\text{N·m}}{0.035 \;\text{m}} = 400 \;\text{N} $$ **Verification:** $F_{column} = MA \times F_{hand} = 10 \times 40 = 400 \;\text{N} \;\;\checkmark$ **Ergonomic Comment:** A mechanical advantage of **10** is comfortable because: - The hand only needs $40 \;\text{N}$ (well within the $230 \;\text{N}$ seated maximum). - Output force of $400 \;\text{N}$ is sufficient to overcome steering resistance. - The ratio balances **precision** (large wheel arc for small column rotation) with **effort economy** (low hand force). --- ### **Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — 14 Marks Alternative Choice]** **Q: (a)** Discuss the design considerations for **foot controls** (pedals). Why are foot controls preferred over hand controls in certain applications? **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **Push Button** control. Differentiate between **flush-type** and **mushroom-type** push buttons. State the standard for emergency stop buttons. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B** **Part (a) — Design Considerations for Foot Controls** Foot controls transfer command from the foot to the machine. They are **preferred over hand controls** when: 1. **Force requirements are high** — foot/leg muscles can generate ~900 N, vs ~230 N for hands. 2. **Hands are occupied** — pilot, driver, surgeon simultaneously operating other devices. 3. **Operation is rhythmic and continuous** — accelerator/brake in driving. 4. **Accidental activation is acceptable** — because foot pressure is naturally modulated. **Design Specifications:** | Parameter | Recommended Value | |---|---| | Pedal angle (from vertical) | $20^\circ$ to $30^\circ$ | | Pedal surface area | Minimum $60 \times 80 \;\text{mm}$ | | Pedal travel | $25$ to $64 \;\text{mm}$ | | Max sustained foot force | $\approx 90 \;\text{N}$ | | Material | Non-slip rubberized surface | | Return mechanism | Spring-loaded (pop-up principle) | **Ergonomic Pitfalls:** - Pedal too far → driver overextends leg → fatigue. - Pedal too close → foot never fully rests → strain. - No non-slip surface → foot slips under load → accident. --- **Part (b) — Push Button Control** A push button is a **discrete, binary control** that closes (or opens) an electrical circuit when pressed. **Block Diagram of Push Button Anatomy:** ```mermaid flowchart TD PB[PUSH BUTTON] --> P1[Cap or Actuator Head] PB --> P2[Return Spring] PB --> P3[Contacts NO and NC] PB --> P4[Mounting Body] P1 --> P1a[Flush head: flat, recessed] P1 --> P1b[Mushroom head: extended, palm-sized] P2 --> P2a[Provides Pop-Up return] P3 --> P3a[Normally Open NO closes on press] P3 --> P3b[Normally Closed NC opens on press] style PB fill:#a3e4d7,stroke:#000 ``` **Difference Between Flush and Mushroom Push Buttons:** | Property | Flush Push Button | Mushroom Push Button | |---|---|---| | Head shape | Flat, recessed | Dome-shaped, protruding | | Activation | Fingertip | Palm or whole-hand | | Use | Routine ON/OFF, mode selection | **Emergency Stop**, critical halt | | Anti-activation | Recess prevents accidental press | Protrusion invites quick strike | | Visual | Low profile | Highly visible | **Standard for Emergency Stop:** According to **ISO 13850** and Indian Standard **IS 60204-1**: - Emergency stop must be **RED** with a **YELLOW background**. - **Mushroom-headed** (palm or fist strike). - Must **mechanically latch** in the pressed position. - Must be **manually reset** (twist-to-release or key-reset). - Must be located within easy reach of the operator's normal working position. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students commonly LOSE marks on Controls questions:** > > 1. **Confusing "mechanical advantage" with "velocity ratio."** MA is force ratio; VR is distance ratio. For an ideal control, they are equal, but students often use the wrong formula. > > 2. **Forgetting units.** Steering-wheel torque must be in **N·m**, force in **N**, length in **meters**. Mixing mm and m is the #1 numerical-error source. > > 3. **Skipping the "ergonomic comment."** KTU expects you to **interpret** the numerical result — e.g., "the MA of 10 is ergonomically acceptable because the hand force 40 N is well within the 230 N maximum." Without this comment, you lose **1–2 marks** even when the math is correct. > > 4. **Misidentifying the control type.** A joystick is NOT the same as a lever. A toggle is NOT the same as a push-button. Use the **exact terminology** in the KTU syllabus. > > 5. **Ignoring "Pop-Up" principle** — any answer describing a control without mentioning its fail-safe return state is incomplete. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 Topic: Controls** - ☐ A **control** is a manually operated device transmitting intent to a machine. - ☐ Controls are classified by **mode** (hand/foot), **function** (discrete/continuous), and **geometry** (linear/rotary/push-pull). - ☐ **Knobs** handle fine tuning; **cranks** handle continuous rotation; **levers** handle high-force angular input; **push buttons** are discrete; **wheels** provide high mechanical advantage. - ☐ Optimal **crank length** = $\frac{1}{4}$ to $\frac{1}{2}$ of forearm length. - ☐ **Mechanical advantage** of a wheel: $MA = \dfrac{R_{wheel}}{r_{hub}}$. - ☐ **Torque** on a knob/wheel: $T = F \times r$. - ☐ **Control–Display ratio** ideal = 1; acceptable range 2–6 for precision. - ☐ **Foot pedal** angle = $20^\circ$–$30^\circ$; travel = $25$–$64 \;\text{mm}$. - ☐ **Pop-Up principle** — controls must return to safe state on release. - ☐ **Emergency stop button** — red, mushroom-headed, mechanically latching, manual reset (ISO 13850). - ☐ **Maximum hand push** = $230 \;\text{N}$; **maximum foot push** = $900 \;\text{N}$. - ☐ **Sterling's compatibility** — control movement must match display direction (spatial, movement, cultural stereotype). - ☐ Always state **units** in numerical answers and **add an ergonomic interpretation**. - ☐ Always mention the **safety aspect** (pop-up, fail-safe, emergency stop) — examiners reward it.

topics.title: Tools and Related Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEAGT831 - HUMAN ENGG AND SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Tools and Related Devices # 🔧 Tools and Related Devices — Module 2 Premium Notes ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) In the context of **Human Engineering (Ergonomics)**, *Tools and Related Devices* refers to the systematic study, design, and evaluation of hand tools, power tools, displays, controls, and workplace equipment such that human physical, sensory, cognitive, and biomechanical capabilities are optimally matched to the demands of the device. The discipline ensures **safety, productivity, comfort, and the reduction of occupational musculoskeletal disorders (MSDs)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Block:** > A *tool* is any device used by a human operator to amplify, extend, or refine a physical capability (force, precision, reach, sensory acuity). The study of tools under Human Engineering is governed by four pillars: **(1) Anthropometry**, **(2) Biomechanics**, **(3) Psychophysics**, and **(4) Safety Engineering**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Hand as the Original "Multi-Tool"** > > Imagine your hand is a master craftsman born without any tools. It can grasp a needle, swing a hammer, type a key, or turn a steering wheel. But give it even a simple wooden spoon and suddenly it can stir, lift, scrape, and measure. Tools are essentially **mechanical "add-ons" for the human body**. A good tool feels like an invisible extension of your hand — you forget it is there. A *badly designed* tool, however, fights your muscles, hurts your wrist, slips when wet, and slowly damages your joints. Human Engineering of tools is the science of making the tool "disappear" into the worker. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants - **Standard Static Hand Grip Strength (Adult Male, P50):** **229 N to 343 N** - **Standard Static Hand Grip Strength (Adult Female, P50):** **138 N to 220 N** - **Maximum Voluntary Torque (Wrist, Male):** **~7–10 N·m** - **Recommended Maximum Hand-Arm Vibration Exposure (ISO 5349, 8-hour A(8)):** **5 m/s²** - **Optimal Power Tool Trigger Force:** **< 10 N** for sustained use - **Standard Pinch Grip Force Threshold for Fatigue:** **~65 N** ### 1.4 Classification of Tools (Foundation Overview) | Category | Examples | Primary Ergonomic Concern | |---|---|---| | Hand Tools | Hammers, screwdrivers, pliers | Grip geometry, vibration, force transmission | | Power Tools | Drills, grinders, sanders | Vibration, noise, trigger force, kickback | | Controls | Knobs, switches, levers, pedals | Resistance, coding, accidental activation | | Displays | Dials, gauges, screens, lights | Readability, mapping, perception latency | | Seating/Support | Chairs, footrests, ladders | Posture, reach envelopes, slip resistance | | PPE-related Devices | Gloves, harnesses | Sensory attenuation, fit, dexterity loss | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hierarchical Tool-Design Principles (KTU Examiner's Priority) The design of any tool is governed by a stack of principles — each builds on the previous. KTU examiners frequently test these in **2-mark and 3-mark short-answer sections**. 1. **Match to Anthropometry (Size Principle):** Dimensions must accommodate the 5th–95th percentile of the target population. 2. **Match to Biomechanics (Posture Principle):** Tools must allow neutral wrist posture (deviation < 15°). 3. **Match to Sensory Capability (Feedback Principle):** Provide adequate tactile, auditory, or visual feedback. 4. **Match to Cognitive Load (Coding Principle):** Shape-coding, color-coding, and labeling must be intuitive. 5. **Safety-by-Design:** Eliminate sharp edges, pinching points, and inadvertent activation. > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for Examiners:** *"A-B-C-D-S"* — *Anthropometry, Biomechanics, Cognition, Display, Safety.* ## 2.2 Types of Grip — A Core KTU Concept The human hand can grasp a tool in fundamentally different biomechanical modes: - **Power Grip:** Entire hand wraps the handle; maximum force (~250–300 N); used in hammering, lifting. - **Precision (Pinch) Grip:** Fingertips only; low force (~20–40 N); used in writing, electronics assembly. - **Hook Grip:** Fingers flex without thumb; sustained holding (carrying bags). - **Pinch Grip Variants:** *Tip pinch*, *Three-jaw chuck pinch*, *Lateral pinch*. ## 2.3 Force and Torque Analysis for Tools ### 2.3.1 Wrench / Screwdriver Torque Equation The torque applied to a fastener via a wrench is: $$\tau = F \times L$$ where $\tau$ is torque (N·m), $F$ is the hand-applied force (N), and $L$ is the lever-arm length (m). ### 2.3.2 Mechanical Advantage of Bent-Handle Tools $$\text{MA} = \frac{L_{\text{effective}}}{L_{\text{direct}}} = \frac{\sin(\theta_{\text{wrist}})}{\sin(\theta_{\text{bend}})}$$ A bent-handle screwdriver (e.g., 23° offset) keeps the wrist in a neutral posture while transmitting greater torque. ### 2.3.3 Vibration Dose Value (VDV) — ISO 5349 $$\text{VDV} = \left[ \int_{0}^{T} a_w(t)^4 \, dt \right]^{1/4}$$ The European Union Vibration Directive uses VDV > **9.1 m/s¹·⁷⁵** as the exposure action value. ### 2.3.4 Reach Envelope (Anthropometric Functional Reach) For a 5th-percentile female seated operator (functional reach forward): $$R_{\text{min}} = L_{\text{shoulder-to-fist}} \approx 0.66 \, \text{m}$$ ### 2.3.5 Snook–Ciriello Maximum Acceptable Force Used for MMH (Manual Material Handling) limits. The maximum acceptable force for a given percentage of the workforce: $$F_{\text{max}} = F_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot f_{\text{actions}}}$$ where $F_0$ is the initial force (N), $k$ is a frequency-decay constant, and $f_{\text{actions}}$ is actions per minute. --- ## 2.4 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application / Boundary | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Wrench torque | $\tau = F \cdot L$ | N·m | Maximum torque without wrist injury: $L \le 0.2\,\text{m}$ for sustained use | | 2 | Mechanical advantage (bent tool) | $\text{MA} = \frac{L_{\text{eff}}}{L_{\text{direct}}}$ | dimensionless | Optimal bend = 23° for screwdrivers | | 3 | Hand grip strength (P50 male) | $F_{\text{grip}} \approx 230\text{–}343$ | N | Limit sustained force to ~15% of MVC | | 4 | Vibration Dose Value | $\text{VDV} = \left[ \int a_w^4 \, dt \right]^{1/4}$ | m/s¹·⁷⁵ | Action value = **9.1**, Limit = **21** | | 5 | Functional reach (5th pct. seated) | $R \approx 0.66$ | m | Display must lie within sagittal reach | | 6 | Recommended trigger force | $F_{\text{trig}} < 10$ | N | ISO 9355-3 guideline | | 7 | Snook–Ciriello force decay | $F_{\text{max}} = F_0 e^{-k f}$ | N | MMH lifting guidelines | | 8 | Optimum handle diameter | $D = 30\text{–}50$ | mm | Power grip; smaller for precision grip | | 9 | Pinch grip fatigue threshold | $F \approx 65$ | N | Above this, MSD risk rises sharply | | 10 | Wrist deviation limit | $\theta \le 15°$ | degrees | Beyond 15°, carpal tunnel pressure ↑ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiners' Hot Tip:** The numbers in **bold** above are the most frequently memorized thresholds in KTU university examinations (Dec 2023, June 2024 patterns). ## 2.5 Display–Control Compatibility (Poulton's Rules) A control's motion should be **compatible** with the display it actuates. Poulton identified: - **Population Stereotype:** Most drivers expect the steering wheel to turn the car *the same way* the wheel is rotated (80%+ compliance). - **Spatial Compatibility:** Right = right; up = up. - **Movement Compatibility:** Up-lever should produce "up" on the display. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive Industry:** Bent-handle screwdrivers reduce assembly-line wrist injuries by up to **40%**. - **Aerospace:** Reach envelopes in cockpit design use 5th-pct female to 95th-pct male dimensions. - **Construction:** Anti-vibration gloves + spring-balanced handles reduce Hand-Arm Vibration Syndrome (HAVS) prevalence. - **Consumer Electronics:** Touch-screen icons must lie within thumb's natural arc (≈ 60° sweep). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I: Maximum Wrench Torque Without Wrist Injury **Problem:** A technician uses a wrench of length $L = 0.25\,\text{m}$ and applies a two-handed grip force. The maximum hand-grip strength of the operator (P50 male) is $F_g = 343\,\text{N}$. Determine the maximum torque deliverable, and check if it exceeds the biomechanical safety limit of $\tau_{\text{max}} = 10\,\text{N·m}$ for sustained wrist neutral posture. ### Step 1 — Identify the governing equation The torque delivered by a wrench equals the perpendicular force times the lever-arm length: $$\tau = F_{\text{applied}} \times L$$ ### Step 2 — Apply the 15% MVC rule For sustained work, ergonomic guidelines limit applied force to 15% of the Maximum Voluntary Contraction (MVC): $$F_{\text{applied}} = 0.15 \times F_g$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute numerical values $$F_{\text{applied}} = 0.15 \times 343\,\text{N} = 51.45\,\text{N}$$ ### Step 4 — Compute the torque $$\tau = 51.45\,\text{N} \times 0.25\,\text{m}$$ $$\tau = 12.86\,\text{N·m}$$ ### Step 5 — Compare with the safety threshold $$\tau_{\text{computed}} = 12.86\,\text{N·m} \;\; > \;\; \tau_{\text{max}} = 10\,\text{N·m}$$ ### Step 6 — Engineering conclusion $$\boxed{\text{The applied torque EXCEEDS the safe threshold by } 28.6\% \Rightarrow \text{Reduce } L \text{ to } 0.19\,\text{m or use a torque-limiting wrench.}}$$ **Conversion Logic:** The single reason torque exceeded limits is the lever arm being too long; halving it brings the operation back into the safe envelope. --- ## 3.2 Derivation II: Mechanical Advantage of a Bent-Handle Screwdriver **Problem:** Show that a bent screwdriver with handle offset $\beta = 23°$ produces a mechanical advantage over a straight screwdriver when the wrist flexion angle is $\theta_w = 30°$. ### Step 1 — Geometry of the bent handle Let the screwdriver shaft make an angle $\beta$ with the handle axis. The "effective" length projected onto the line of force is: $$L_{\text{eff}} = L \cdot \cos(\beta)$$ ### Step 2 — Effective force component For the straight screwdriver (wrist flexed by $\theta_w$): $$F_{\text{eff,straight}} = F_{\text{hand}} \cdot \cos(\theta_w)$$ ### Step 3 — Effective force with bent handle The bent handle aligns the forearm with the shaft, so the effective angle becomes $\theta_w - \beta$: $$F_{\text{eff,bent}} = F_{\text{hand}} \cdot \cos(\theta_w - \beta)$$ ### Step 4 — Mechanical advantage ratio $$\text{MA} = \frac{F_{\text{eff,bent}}}{F_{\text{eff,straight}}} = \frac{\cos(\theta_w - \beta)}{\cos(\theta_w)}$$ ### Step 5 — Substitute $\theta_w = 30°$, $\beta = 23°$ $$\text{MA} = \frac{\cos(30° - 23°)}{\cos(30°)} = \frac{\cos(7°)}{\cos(30°)}$$ $$\text{MA} = \frac{0.9925}{0.8660} \approx 1.146$$ ### Step 6 — Final result $$\boxed{\text{MA} \approx 1.15 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{14.6\% more torque at the same wrist strain.}}$$ **Conversion Logic:** A small geometric change at the handle dramatically improves usable torque — this is why every professional screwdriver has an offset handle. --- ## 3.3 Derivation III: Vibration Dose Value (VDV) — Tool Exposure Check **Problem:** A worker uses a jackhammer producing a weighted RMS acceleration $a_w = 12\,\text{m/s}^2$ for a daily duration $T = 2\,\text{hours} = 7200\,\text{s}$. Compute VDV and compare with the EU limit (21 m/s¹·⁷⁵). ### Step 1 — Constant-amplitude simplification Since $a_w(t)$ is approximately constant: $$\text{VDV} = \left[ a_w^4 \cdot T \right]^{1/4} = a_w \cdot T^{1/4}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute $T^{1/4}$ $$T^{1/4} = 7200^{0.25} = (7200)^{0.25}$$ $$\log_{10}(7200) = 3.857 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; 0.25 \times 3.857 = 0.964$$ $$10^{0.964} \approx 9.21$$ ### Step 3 — Compute VDV $$\text{VDV} = 12 \times 9.21 = 110.5\,\text{m/s}^{1.75}$$ ### Step 4 — Compare with limits $$\text{VDV} = 110.5 \;\; \gg \;\; 21 \;\; (\text{Limit})$$ ### Step 5 — Engineering conclusion $$\boxed{\text{Exposure is } \approx 5.26\times \text{the legal limit} \Rightarrow \text{Rotate operator, use anti-vibration tool, or shorten duration.}}$$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Tool Type vs. Ergonomic Risk | Tool Type | Primary Risk | Critical Design Variable | KTU-Relevant Number | |---|---|---|---| | Hammer | Wrist/elbow tendonitis | Handle mass, swing arc | $m \le 0.45\,\text{kg}$ sustained | | Screwdriver (straight) | Carpal tunnel | Handle diameter, bend angle | $D = 30\text{–}50\,\text{mm}$, $\beta = 23°$ | | Power drill | HAVS, noise | Vibration isolation, grip | $a_w \le 5\,\text{m/s}^2$ | | Pliers | Hand fatigue, pinch | Spring return, handle span | Span = 60–90 mm | | Grinding wheel | Eye injury, vibration | Guard, kickback brake | Mandatory guard per ISO | | Ladder | Falls | Slip resistance, angle | 4:1 ladder ratio | | Control knob | Inadvertent activation | Detents, coding | Resistance = 1–3 N·m | --- # 4.
